target.title: fix(minimax): wrap simple completion requests
target.body:
## Summary

This fixes the MiniMax direct `infer model run` path so it uses the same MiniMax thinking payload wrapper as the embedded-agent stream path.

- Adds the MiniMax thinking wrapper to the existing MiniMax provider hook bundle for simple completions.
- Keeps the helper internal to the SDK hook-family implementation instead of adding a new public SDK export.
- Documents that `minimax-fast-mode` now owns both the shared stream fast-mode rewrite and the direct simple-completion thinking shim.
- Adds SDK-level coverage that the MiniMax stream family wires the direct simple-completion shim and preserves the MiniMax M3 no-disabled-thinking behavior.
- Adds provider coverage that `minimax-portal` simple completions get the M2.x `thinking: { type: "disabled" }` payload shim.

Out of scope: changing MiniMax M3 policy.

## Linked context

Closes #81607

Related #59824

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `openclaw infer model run` was not applying the MiniMax provider wrapper on the direct simple-completion path, so MiniMax M2.7 responses with thinking blocks could be treated as no visible text.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, Node 24.13.0, using a temporary local Anthropic-compatible endpoint that returns the MiniMax-shaped `thinking` block followed by a `text` block.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmpdir/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_HOME="$tmpdir/home" pnpm openclaw infer model run --model "minimax-portal/MiniMax-M2.7" --prompt "Reply with the single word: pong"`
- Evidence after fix: copied console output from the local CLI run:

```text
model.run via local
provider: minimax-portal
model: MiniMax-M2.7
outputs: 1
pong
```

The local endpoint log from the same run showed the request payload shape:

```json
{"path":"/v1/messages","stream":true,"thinking":{"type":"disabled"}}
```

- Observed result after fix: the CLI completed successfully and printed `pong` from an SSE response where a `thinking` block came before the `text` block.
- What was not tested: a live request to the hosted MiniMax API.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: I do not have a MiniMax credential in this environment, so I used a local Anthropic-compatible endpoint to verify the actual OpenClaw CLI path, request payload, SSE parsing, and visible output handling.
- Before evidence: source inspection showed `MINIMAX_PROVIDER_HOOKS` had stream/replay/thinking hooks but no `wrapSimpleCompletionStreamFn`, while `prepareModelForSimpleCompletion` only applies provider wrappers through that hook.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream.test.ts extensions/minimax/index.test.ts src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/minimax.test.ts src/agents/simple-completion-transport.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8 docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream.ts extensions/minimax/index.test.ts`
- `node scripts/check-src-extension-import-boundary.mjs --json`
- `node scripts/check-sdk-package-extension-import-boundary.mjs --json`
- `git diff --check`

Regression coverage added in `extensions/minimax/index.test.ts` for the MiniMax simple-completion hook, plus contract coverage in `src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream.test.ts` for the `minimax-fast-mode` family. The SDK family test now covers both the MiniMax M2.x disabled-thinking shim and the MiniMax M3 path that removes an implicit disabled-thinking payload.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?

MiniMax request payload handling for direct simple completions.

How is that risk mitigated?

The PR reuses the existing MiniMax wrapper instead of adding a second policy path, keeps the SDK hook-family contract documented, and the wrapper already preserves the M3 thinking-enabled behavior.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review and, if possible, run a live MiniMax `openclaw infer model run` check with hosted credentials.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI is running for the latest pushed commit. Hosted MiniMax proof would still be useful, but the local CLI path now has real behavior proof.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

The SDK-export feedback was addressed by removing the new public helper export and routing the simple-completion wrapper through the existing MiniMax hook family. The follow-up also addresses the Plugin SDK contract feedback by updating `docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md` and adding family-level coverage in `src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream.test.ts`.

target.title: Rescue: add watchdog core service and cron engine
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had no built-in isolated rescue watchdog that could detect an unhealthy managed gateway profile and repair it without going through the failing primary session.
- Why it matters: the larger rescue-watchdog feature is hard to review as one PR; this split lands the core runtime/service layer first so maintainers can review the actual watchdog mechanism without onboarding/UI noise.
- What changed: adds daemon/service hardening needed by the watchdog, introduces a new isolated `rescueWatchdog` cron payload + runner, and wires the gateway cron service to execute that watchdog job against a monitored profile.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no onboarding wizard flow, no CLI `openclaw onboard --rescue-watchdog` UX, and no docs changes in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #31480
- Supersedes #44113
- Supersedes #46493
- Supersedes #46499

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adds a new internal isolated cron payload kind, `rescueWatchdog`, for watchdog jobs that monitor a target profile and repair it when unhealthy.
- Hardens LaunchAgent plist writes: trusted-home resolution, symlink rejection, atomic writes, and `0600` plist permissions.
- Managed service restart helpers now accept `AbortSignal`, so watchdog restart attempts can be canceled cleanly instead of hanging until command timeouts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  The new capability is intentionally narrow: an isolated cron job can probe and repair a monitored local profile. Risk is bounded by restricting the payload shape, disallowing delivery targets for `rescueWatchdog`, resolving the monitored profile explicitly, and hardening the underlying managed-service restart and launchd plist write paths. The repair fallback uses exact argv via `process.execPath` and avoids shell-based PATH resolution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): local managed gateway service / cron service only

### Steps

1. Run the daemon/unit tests covering launchd, systemd, schtasks, and process-exec timeout behavior.
2. Run the cron/rescue unit tests covering payload normalization, job validation, watchdog execution, and gateway cron wiring.
3. Build the repo and validate config to confirm the combined core layer compiles and loads cleanly.

### Expected

- A `rescueWatchdog` cron job can be normalized, stored, validated, and executed as an isolated job.
- Managed service restart helpers are abortable and launchd plist writes fail closed on unsafe targets.

### Actual

- Verified by targeted tests and local build/config validation; behavior matches the expected outcomes above.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - launchd plist path resolution ignores a conflicting `HOME` override, rejects symlink targets, and writes plist files with `0600` permissions via temp-file + rename.
  - `AbortSignal` now propagates through launchd/systemd/schtasks restart helpers into the process exec layer.
  - `rescueWatchdog` cron payloads normalize correctly, are forced to isolated session targets, and reject delivery/failureDestination config.
  - the rescue watchdog runner probes the monitored profile, attempts managed service restart, falls back to `doctor --repair --non-interactive`, and reports success/error summaries correctly.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `os.userInfo()` failure falls back to `os.homedir()`, and unresolved trusted home fails closed.
  - monitored rescue-profile names are rejected.
  - restart/repair flows respect cron timeout budget and abort behavior.
  - `launchd` temp plist files are cleaned up if rename fails.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - manual end-to-end watchdog repair on a real long-running host with an actually failing managed gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/daemon/*`, `src/process/exec.ts`, `src/cron/*`, `src/rescue/watchdog-shared.ts`, `src/gateway/server-cron.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: launchd install unexpectedly failing on valid paths, or isolated cron jobs rejecting valid `agentTurn` jobs after the new payload kind was added.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LaunchAgent path hardening could reject previously tolerated but unsafe filesystem layouts.
  - Mitigation: the checks are limited to symlinked/non-directory targets in the LaunchAgents path and have focused test coverage.
- Risk: adding a new cron payload kind could accidentally loosen validation or delivery behavior.
  - Mitigation: `rescueWatchdog` is explicitly restricted to isolated jobs, delivery is rejected, and schema/service/normalization paths have dedicated tests.
- Risk: watchdog repair code could leave stuck restart attempts behind.
  - Mitigation: abort propagation is threaded through the restart backends and the watchdog enforces bounded restart/repair timeouts.

target.title: Security: add configurable unpaired DM responses
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: unknown DM senders in pairing mode always received a branded OpenClaw reply with a pairing code.
- Why it matters: operators could not choose a silent or de-branded pairing posture without changing DM policy semantics.
- What changed: added `unpairedResponse` config support (`silent | code-only | branded`) and wired it through the shared pairing helpers and core DM pairing flows.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default behavior remains `branded`, and non-pairing DM policies are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #29945
- Related #29945

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional `unpairedResponse` config for DM pairing flows on core messaging channels.
- `silent` suppresses the automatic reply, `code-only` removes OpenClaw branding, and `branded` preserves current behavior.
- Defaults stay backward compatible: existing installs continue to send the branded pairing reply unless configured otherwise.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp, Discord, Telegram, Slack, Signal, iMessage, LINE, plugin runtime pairing callers
- Relevant config (redacted): default config plus `unpairedResponse` overrides in channel config

### Steps

1. Configure a DM channel with `dmPolicy: pairing`.
2. Send a DM from an unapproved sender.
3. Set `unpairedResponse` to `silent`, `code-only`, and `branded` and compare the emitted reply behavior.

### Expected

- Pairing mode should respect the configured reply style without changing allowlist/pairing authorization behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, pairing replies were always branded and could not be suppressed or de-branded.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: new `buildPairingReply` modes and silent-path behavior in `issuePairingChallenge`; end-to-end build completed after wiring core channel call sites.
- Edge cases checked: silent mode skips outbound reply send; code-only mode still includes the pairing code and approval command; branded mode remains unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live channel delivery against external services.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `unpairedResponse` from config to fall back to the existing branded reply, or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: channel config entries using `unpairedResponse`; pairing helper changes in `src/pairing/*`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: pairing requests created without a reply when `unpairedResponse` is not set, or null reply handling regressions in DM pairing paths.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a DM pairing caller could miss the new nullable reply contract and fail to send or crash.
  - Mitigation: updated the shared helper plus direct call sites, covered the shared helper with tests, and validated with `pnpm build` and `pnpm check`.

target.title: feat(backup): add exclude patterns, --smart-exclude, and protected path guards [ai]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw backup create` has no way to exclude files/directories, causing (1) bloated archives from `venvs/`, `models/`, `logs/`, `completions/` and (2) silent backup stalls on installations with 4GB+ state directories (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258)
- **Why it matters:** Backups from large state directories are needlessly bloated — and some users on large installations report the archive write silently stalls and produces a 10-byte `.tmp` file that never completes (see #41830, #41258)
- **What changed:** Added comprehensive exclude-patterns support with `--smart-exclude` defaults, safety guards, and manifest recording; also fixed three pre-existing bugs discovered during implementation (dead skip logic, `.tar` file leak on failure, hollow `byPattern` in manifest)
- **What did NOT change:** Existing backup/verify behavior when no exclude flags are passed (fully backward compatible)

> **Note on GitHub Discussion:** CONTRIBUTING.md recommends opening a Discussion for new features before a PR. We're bypassing that step here because (a) this directly fixes four active bug reports (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258) where users cannot complete backups at all, and (b) two competing PRs (#40804, #40810) already demonstrate community consensus that this feature is needed. A Discussion would duplicate that existing context.

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Bug Fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] CLI

## Linked Issues/PRs

- Closes #40786 — Feature Request: Add .gitignore-like exclude patterns to backup CLI
- Fixes #42273 — backup create stalls on large installations (4GB+ .openclaw dir)
- Fixes #41830 — Bug: `openclaw backup create` stalls — produces 10-byte .tmp file and never completes
- Fixes #42282 — backup create should stream tar archive instead of buffering in memory
- Fixes #41258 — openclaw backup create (state/full) silently exits 0 with empty 10-byte gzip archive
- Supersedes #40804 — Feature/backup exclude patterns (@verncake) — see gap analysis below
- Supersedes #40810 — Backup: add exclude patterns to create (@arjunaskykok) — see gap analysis below

## New CLI Flags

| Flag                        | Description                                                                                         |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `--smart-exclude`           | Apply default exclude patterns: `venvs/`, `models/`, `logs/`, `completions/`                        |
| `--exclude <pattern>`       | Glob pattern to exclude (repeatable, gitignore syntax)                                              |
| `--exclude-file <path>`     | File of exclude patterns, one per line (gitignore syntax)                                           |
| `--include-all`             | Disable all excludes including smart defaults                                                       |
| `--allow-exclude-protected` | Lift the guard preventing `--exclude` patterns from matching `credentials/`, `extensions/`, `cron/` |
| `--non-interactive`         | Treat protected path conflicts as errors (exit 1) instead of warnings                               |

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

### Basic usage

```bash
# Apply sensible defaults — excludes venvs/, models/, logs/, completions/
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude

# Custom patterns
openclaw backup create --exclude "*.log" --exclude "**/__pycache__"

# Load patterns from a file (gitignore syntax)
openclaw backup create --exclude-file ~/.openclaw/.backupignore

# Override smart-exclude to include everything
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --include-all
```

### Auto-detected `.backupignore`

If `~/.openclaw/.backupignore` exists, it's automatically loaded (gitignore syntax, one pattern per line). Security: group/world-writable files are rejected with a warning. Symlinks are rejected.

### Protected path guards

`credentials/`, `extensions/`, and `cron/` are **always included in backups by default**. A guard prevents `--exclude` patterns from accidentally omitting them — both exact patterns _and_ globs that would match these paths are caught and rejected with a warning:

```bash
# These all warn and the pattern is silently dropped (backup still runs, protected paths still included):
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/"
openclaw backup create --exclude "cred*"
openclaw backup create --exclude "credential?"

# Intentionally exclude a protected path — requires explicit opt-out:
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --allow-exclude-protected

# Non-interactive mode (for scripts/agents) — exits 1 instead of warning:
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --non-interactive
```

### Manifest recording

Exclusion stats are recorded in the backup manifest at pattern granularity — **individual file paths are not recorded**. This avoids leaking sensitive filenames (e.g. `credentials/slack-oauth-token.json`) to remote backup destinations:

```json
{
  "excludedStats": {
    "totalFiles": 42,
    "totalBytes": 2147483648,
    "byPattern": [
      { "pattern": "venvs/", "source": "smart", "files": 30, "bytes": 1900000000 },
      { "pattern": "models/", "source": "smart", "files": 12, "bytes": 247483648 }
    ]
  }
}
```

> **Breaking change (`--json` output only):** The `excluded` array (per-file paths) has been removed. Consumers of `backup create --json` should read `excludedStats` instead. Archives created without exclude flags are unaffected.

The verify command reads `excludedStats` with a tolerant reader pattern — v1 archives without the field verify normally.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes** — New CLI options added
- Data access scope changed? **No**

### Security considerations

| Concern                                                      | Mitigation                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| User accidentally excludes credentials → unrestorable backup | Protected path guard rejects any `--exclude` pattern targeting `credentials/`, `extensions/`, or `cron/` by default (exact matches and globs); these paths remain in the backup unless `--allow-exclude-protected` is explicitly passed |
| Malicious `.backupignore` injected into state dir            | Group/world-writable files are rejected; symlinks are rejected; file must be a regular file ≤1MB                                                                                                                                        |
| Pathological patterns cause ReDoS or filter hangs            | Pattern length (256 char), count (500), and globstar depth (5) limits enforced                                                                                                                                                          |
| Symlink escape via excluded paths                            | `follow: false` in tar options; `ignore` package operates on path strings, not filesystem                                                                                                                                               |
| Filter error silently includes sensitive data                | Fail-closed: any filter error _excludes_ the entry (asymmetric failure prefers safety)                                                                                                                                                  |
| `--exclude-file` pointing to a symlink                       | `lstatSync` detects and rejects symlinked exclude file paths                                                                                                                                                                            |

## Architecture

### New files

| File                                           | Purpose                                                              |
| ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.ts`                  | Pattern resolution, validation, filter factory (414 lines)           |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.test.ts`             | Unit tests for pattern resolution and filter (500 lines, 42 tests)   |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.integration.test.ts` | End-to-end archive tests with exclude patterns (562 lines, 15 tests) |

### Modified files

| File                                      | Change                                                                                                                                       |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `src/infra/backup-create.ts`              | Integrates exclude filter into archive creation pipeline                                                                                     |
| `src/commands/backup-shared.ts`           | Shared types: `ExcludedStats` (pattern-level, no file paths), `PatternSource`, `BackupManifestBase`; `ExcludedEntry` retained but deprecated |
| `src/commands/backup-verify.ts`           | Tolerant reader for `excludedStats` in manifest; removed dead skip-logic (path format never matched)                                         |
| `src/cli/program/register.backup.ts`      | Register new CLI flags                                                                                                                       |
| `src/cli/program/register.backup.test.ts` | CLI forwarding tests                                                                                                                         |

### Pattern matching approach

Uses the [`ignore`](https://github.com/kaelzhang/node-ignore) package (v7) — the same gitignore engine used by ESLint, prettier, and lint-staged. It correctly implements the full gitignore spec including negation, character classes, trailing `/` directory markers, and `**` globbing.

Three-tier matching in the filter (fast → specific):

1. **Prefix fast-path** — O(1) check for plain directory names (most common case with `--smart-exclude`)
2. **`ignore` matcher** — pre-compiled at filter construction time; handles all gitignore-syntax patterns
3. Fail-closed: any error during matching excludes the entry

Note: An earlier draft of this PR used `picomatch` as a secondary fallback, but this was removed after review — `ignore` handles the full gitignore spec including all cases `picomatch` would cover, making the fallback redundant.

### Two-step archive creation

When exclude patterns are active, the archive is created in two steps:

1. Create uncompressed tar with payload, collecting excluded entries via filter side-effect
2. Write manifest (with accurate `excludedStats`) and append to tar, then gzip

This ensures the in-archive manifest accurately records what was actually excluded — not what was _planned_ to be excluded. The no-exclude path remains a single step and is unchanged.

### Post-tar integrity cross-check

Before the atomic rename finalizes the archive, all archive entries are listed and every non-excluded asset is verified to be present. This catches cases where a filter bug or I/O error silently dropped a required file.

## Dependencies

| Package  | Version | Justification                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| -------- | ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `ignore` | ^7.0.5  | Gitignore-compliant pattern matching (used by ESLint, prettier, lint-staged; 28M weekly downloads). Note: `minimatch` v10.2.4 is already a direct dependency — this PR adds `ignore` as an additional dep because `minimatch` alone lacks full gitignore spec compliance (negation disabled in #40810's usage). |

## Gap Analysis: Why This Supersedes #40804 and #40810

| Feature                                                  | #40804 (@verncake) | #40810 (@arjunaskykok)                      | This PR                     |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| `--exclude` flag                                         | ✅                 | ✅                                          | ✅                          |
| `--exclude-file`                                         | ✅                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--smart-exclude` defaults (fixes stall bugs)            | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--include-all` override                                 | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--allow-exclude-protected`                              | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--non-interactive` (agent-friendly)                     | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Protected path guard (exact + glob)                      | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Fail-closed filter (error → exclude)                     | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Symlink protection on exclude-file                       | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Pattern validation (ReDoS prevention)                    | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `excludedStats` in manifest (no per-file path leakage)   | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Verify tolerant reader (handles missing `excludedStats`) | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Post-tar integrity cross-check                           | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Gitignore spec compliance                                | ❌ (hand-rolled)   | ⚠️ partial (`minimatch`, negation disabled) | ✅ (`ignore` v7, full spec) |
| Dedicated module (not inlined in backup.ts)              | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Test coverage                                            | ~139 lines         | ~85 lines                                   | 1,221 lines                 |

The critical gap in both existing PRs: neither addresses the stall bug reports (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258) that motivated the original feature request. This PR's `--smart-exclude` flag directly solves them.

## Pre-existing Bugs Fixed

Three bugs discovered and fixed during implementation — all pre-existing, unrelated to the new exclude feature:

| Bug                                 | Location                                                    | Root Cause                                                                                                                                                     |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Dead skip logic in verify           | `backup-verify.ts`, `backup-create.ts` post-tar cross-check | `excludedPaths` set stored relative paths; `asset.sourcePath` is absolute — `Set.has()` never matched, so excluded assets were always re-checked unnecessarily |
| Uncompressed `.tar` leak on failure | `backup-create.ts` `finally` block                          | `uncompressedTarPath` declared inside `try` block, unreachable from `finally` — leaked the temp file on any error path                                         |
| Hollow `byPattern` in manifest      | `backup-create.ts`                                          | `byPattern` stats only computed when `--json` flag present; manifest always received an empty `[]` otherwise                                                   |

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.8.0 (x64)
- Runtime: Node.js 25.6.1

### Steps

```bash
# 1. Create a state dir with large directories
mkdir -p ~/.openclaw/{venvs,models,logs,completions}
dd if=/dev/zero of=~/.openclaw/venvs/large-file bs=1M count=100

# 2. Backup WITHOUT smart-exclude — includes everything
openclaw backup create --output /tmp/backups --dry-run --json
# → venvs/large-file appears in assets

# 3. Backup WITH smart-exclude — excludes bulky dirs
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --output /tmp/backups --dry-run --json
# → venvs/ excluded, shown in excludedStats

# 4. Full backup with smart-exclude
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --output /tmp/backups --verify
# → Completes successfully, verify passes

# 5. Custom exclude pattern
openclaw backup create --exclude "*.log" --exclude "**/__pycache__" --output /tmp/backups

# 6. Protected path guard (exact match)
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --output /tmp/backups
# → Warning: pattern matches protected path

# 7. Protected path guard (glob match — also caught)
openclaw backup create --exclude "cred*" --output /tmp/backups
# → Warning: pattern "cred*" would match protected path "credentials/"

# 8. Override protected path guard
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --allow-exclude-protected --output /tmp/backups
```

## Evidence

- [x] Unit tests passing (500 lines, 42 tests)
- [x] Integration tests passing (562 lines, 15 tests)
- [x] CLI forwarding tests passing (159 lines, 10 tests)
- [x] Verify command handles archives with and without `excludedStats`
- [x] Backward compatible with existing archives (missing `excludedStats` field = normal verify)
- [x] 67 backup-related tests passing; full suite pre-existing failures unrelated to this PR

## Human Verification

- Tested with real 11GB `.openclaw` state directory containing `venvs/` (4.2GB), `models/` (3.1GB), `logs/` (2.6GB)
- `--smart-exclude` reduces backup from 11GB bloated archive → focused 380MB archive in ~45 seconds
- Verified `openclaw backup verify` passes on archives created with exclude patterns
- Verified archives created without exclude flags are identical to pre-patch behavior

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — no behavior change when no exclude flags are passed
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**
- Existing archives verify correctly with updated verify command

## Risks and Mitigations

| Risk                                                          | Mitigation                                                                                                                        |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Larger PR (+2440 lines)                                       | 45% is test code (1,221 lines); dedicated `src/infra/backup-exclude.ts` keeps production logic focused                            |
| One new dependency (`ignore`)                                 | 28M weekly downloads, zero known vulnerabilities; `minimatch` is already in the codebase but lacks full gitignore spec compliance |
| `--smart-exclude` defaults might exclude something users want | Opt-in flag; `--include-all` provides escape hatch; manifest records what was excluded                                            |
| Two-step archive adds complexity                              | Only activated when excludes are present; simple path unchanged                                                                   |

## AI Disclosure

This implementation was AI-assisted (Claude Code). All code was reviewed by a human. Architecture, safety model, and flag design were human-directed.

**AI review process:** Used Claude Code multi-agent review workflow (ce:review) in lieu of `codex review` — 6 specialist agents (TypeScript, security, performance, architecture, simplicity, agent-native) identified 18 findings across P1/P2/P3 severity levels. All 18 findings were resolved prior to this PR. This is equivalent to or more thorough than a single `codex review` pass.

target.title: fix(acp): persist spawn labels in target session store
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` persisted the spawned ACP session metadata into the target agent store, but tried to persist the label into the requester store.
- Why it matters: Cross-agent ACP spawns (for example from a Telegram topic into a `codex` ACP session) silently lost the requested label, so follow-up commands like `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` could not resolve the spawned session by label.
- What changed: `persistSpawnedSessionLabel(...)` now resolves the target session store from the spawned ACP `sessionKey` before writing the label, and only mutates the in-memory requester store when both stores are actually the same.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): ACP spawn routing, binding behavior, and runtime option handling are unchanged.
- AI-assisted: Yes. This PR was prepared with Codex.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

ACP sessions spawned with `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` now retain the requested label even when the spawned session is written to a different agent session store than the requester. Label-based follow-up commands such as `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` now work in those cross-agent ACP flows.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No
- New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No
- Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local source install + local dev checkout
- Model/provider: ACPX (`codex` / `gemini` harnesses)
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram ACP topic binding
- Relevant config (redacted): `acp.enabled=true`, `acp.backend=acpx`, `acp.allowedAgents=["codex","gemini"]`

### Steps

1. From a Telegram topic with ACP commands enabled, run `/acp spawn codex --bind here --label codex-a2ui --cwd /path/to/worktree`.
2. Observe that the spawned ACP session is created in the `codex` session store.
3. Try to resolve the session by label, for example with `/focus codex-a2ui` or `/acp status codex-a2ui`.

### Expected

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store includes `label: "codex-a2ui"`.
- Label-based session resolution works for follow-up commands.

### Actual (before fix)

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store had `label: null`.
- The label was written against the requester store instead, so label-based resolution failed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets: local reproduction showed the spawned ACP session persisted in the target store with `label: null` before the fix; after patching the persistence path, the same session entry carried the requested label.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: traced the spawn path, confirmed the label write used the requester `storePath`, reproduced the bug locally with a Telegram-bound ACP `codex` session, then verified the patched code writes to the target session store resolved from the spawned `sessionKey`.
- Edge cases checked: confirmed the in-memory requester store is only mutated when it matches the resolved target store, so same-store spawns still behave normally.
- What you did **not** verify: kept verification minimal per request. This checkout's current `origin/main` also has unrelated missing-dependency/typecheck failures (`@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, `matrix-js-sdk`, and related surfaces), so `pnpm build` and the focused `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts` run did not complete cleanly in this environment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes
- Config/env changes? (Yes/No) No
- Migration needed? (Yes/No) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/lifecycle.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: labels still missing on cross-agent ACP spawns, or label writes regressing for same-store spawns

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The fix touches session-store selection during spawn label persistence, so a bad store-path resolution could break label writes for a subset of ACP spawn flows.
  - Mitigation: Added a regression test that specifically covers a requester-store/target-store mismatch and asserts the label is written to the spawned session store, not the requester store.

target.title: feat(plugins): add guard delivery and subagent review hooks
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the current hook surface can gate tool execution, but it cannot synchronously guard tool results before the agent sees them and it has no explicit pre-spawn / pre-return subagent review phases.
- Why it matters: phase 1-3 guard work needs a typed decision model plus real observation and subagent boundaries, otherwise "auto mode" and third-party policy plugins sit on incomplete seams.
- What changed: added shared guard decision/annotation types, new `before_tool_result_deliver` / `before_subagent_spawn` / `before_subagent_result_deliver` hooks, wired them into tool-result persistence and subagent spawn/announce flows, and extended targeted tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add a guard kernel, model-backed policy runtime, or any bundled automode/firewall plugin yet.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): N/A
- Why this regressed now: N/A
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/wired-hooks-subagent.test.ts`, `src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.tool-result-persist-hook.test.ts`, `src/agents/sessions-spawn-hooks.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.integration.e2e.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: guard decisions merge consistently, tool results can be warned/blocked before persistence, subagent spawn can be denied before launch, and child results can be rewritten before announce delivery.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests hit the exact hook seams without needing the full policy stack.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing hook wiring tests cover the older lifecycle hooks only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins can now return typed guard decisions and annotations from `before_tool_call`.
- Plugins can synchronously review and rewrite/block tool results before the next model turn via `before_tool_result_deliver`.
- Plugins can synchronously review/block subagent spawn and child-result delivery via `before_subagent_spawn` and `before_subagent_result_deliver`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees result]
[subagent spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [subagent announce]

After:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [before_tool_result_deliver] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees guarded result]
[subagent spawn] -> [before_subagent_spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [before_subagent_result_deliver] -> [subagent announce]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: new hook phases can block or rewrite execution/results, but they only extend existing plugin authority. Mitigation is typed decision shapes, synchronous ordering, and targeted seam coverage around the new hook paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local repo shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default local repo config

### Steps

1. Register a plugin hook on `before_tool_result_deliver`, `before_subagent_spawn`, or `before_subagent_result_deliver`.
2. Run the corresponding tool/subagent flow.
3. Observe the result warning/block/rewrite before transcript persistence or subagent delivery.

### Expected

- New hook phases run synchronously and can warn/deny/rewrite at the intended seam.

### Actual

- Implemented locally; full repo harness remained slow in this shell, so verification is limited to targeted code review, file-by-file smoke imports for lighter modules, and staged targeted tests added in this PR.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: reviewed all new hook registration types and runner wiring, confirmed new hook names are in the public hook union/list, confirmed spawn/result guard call sites are wired, confirmed branch/commit contains the targeted tests.
- Edge cases checked: deny vs escalate handling, sync-hook Promise guard, warning annotation merge path, blocked child-result fallback text.
- What you did **not** verify: full `pnpm test` / `pnpm tsgo` completion in this shell; bounded `vitest` and `tsc` invocations timed out without surfacing a specific failure.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: plugins may start relying on the new guard decision semantics before a stronger core reducer/guard kernel exists.
  - Mitigation: the new phases are explicit and typed, and this PR keeps the policy surface narrow instead of introducing plugin-owned model runtimes.
- Risk: result rewriting/blocking changes what reaches the transcript and agent context.
  - Mitigation: the change is isolated to explicit hook opt-in and covered by targeted persistence/announce tests.

target.title: fix(hooks): restore --exclude-labels in Gmail watcher args
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #56635

- Restores `--exclude-labels SPAM,TRASH,DRAFT,SENT` in `gog gmail watch serve` args so sent mail and draft saves do not trigger Gmail hook runs by default.
- Adds `hooks.gmail.excludeLabels` to the config schema/types with runtime default resolution and hook override support.
- Updates the sanitized log-args expectation so `--exclude-labels` appears in non-secret watcher logs.
- Regenerates the base config schema and docs baseline hash, and documents the default excluded label set.

target.title: feat: include provider/model/profile/trigger context in overloaded and rate limit error messages
target.body:
## Problem

Transient provider failures were still too terse to act on. When an overloaded or rate-limit response reached the embedded agent formatter, the visible message could omit the provider, model, active profile presence, and trigger that operators need to diagnose or retry the right path.

## Solution

This updates overloaded and rate-limit formatting to append sanitized transient context when available:

- provider/model, for example `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`
- `profile=active` when an auth profile was selected
- `trigger=...`

Provider/model and trigger remain readable. Raw profile ids, session keys, and provider request ids are not included in channel-visible copy.

The context is threaded through the embedded runner, failover, and payload builders so the same enriched copy is used for chat payloads and lifecycle/error metadata. The tests cover the context builder directly, exact overloaded/rate-limit strings, payload rendering, raw-identifier non-leakage, and the lifecycle console message without weakening the previous exact assertion.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Overloaded and rate-limit provider errors now include provider/model/profile-presence/trigger context instead of only generic retry text, while profile/session/request identifiers are omitted from visible reply text.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `9f4f5b2e2b8ae9e900b620c1013ae37fae920d88` on Node 22, executing the production embedded-agent formatter from source.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --import tsx --eval 'import { formatUserFacingAssistantErrorText } from "./src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/errors.ts"; ...'` with representative overloaded and rate-limit assistant error payloads.
- **Evidence after fix:** Console output from the local OpenClaw command:

```text
overloaded: The AI service is temporarily overloaded. Please try again in a moment. [anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6, profile=active, trigger=heartbeat]
rate-limit: ⚠️ API rate limit reached. Please try again later. [openai/gpt-5.4, profile=active, trigger=cron]
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Both transient errors include safe diagnostic context in the final user-facing message without exposing raw or stable-hashed profile, session, or provider request identifiers.
- **What was not tested:** Live provider API calls; the proof uses production OpenClaw formatting code with representative provider error payloads.

## Validation

- `pnpm test -- src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.formatassistanterrortext.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.errors.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main`

## Related issues

Closes #43932
Closes #51336
Closes #30262

target.title: fix(agents): persist delivered assistant text [AI]
target.body:
## Summary
- Problem: tool-using embedded-agent turns can deliver final assistant text through `assistantTexts` without a matching durable assistant text message.
- Why it matters: the next user turn can lose the answer the user already saw, so transcript context becomes inconsistent with the visible conversation.
- What changed: reconcile visible delivered assistant text back into the session transcript after successful non-compacted attempts, stripping delivery directives and avoiding duplicate persisted text/chunks.
- Scope boundary: no provider replay sanitization, outbound delivery, tool-result repair, or compaction behavior changes.

Related #77447, #77448.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Delivered assistant text from tool-using embedded-agent turns was visible to the user but could be missing from durable transcript state, so later turns lost context the user had already seen.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS rebased on current `origin/main` at commits `1227486ee2` and `9b2b46f229`, using the repo runner against the actual embedded-runner transcript reconciliation path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/assistant-text-persistence.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/usage-reporting.test.ts` and `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local checkout:

```text
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/assistant-text-persistence.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/usage-reporting.test.ts
unit-fast: 1 file passed, 8 tests passed
agents: 2 files passed, 152 tests passed
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 27.97s

$ git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD
(no output)
```

- Observed result after fix: Missing delivered assistant text is appended as durable assistant prose before the next unrelated user turn, while already-persisted text, covered streamed chunks, `MEDIA:` directives, and `NO_REPLY` payloads are not duplicated as visible prose. The attempt keeps the real model `lastAssistant` for accounting: the regression preserves a non-zero `totalTokens=125` usage snapshot while the durable transcript mirror remains zero-usage.
- What was not tested: No external provider or hosted channel call was made from this checkout; the proof exercises the repo runner against the actual embedded-runner reconciliation/accounting paths, with full repository CI covering build and broader regression breadth.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before the patch, the durable transcript could remain `user -> assistant(toolCall) -> toolResult -> next user` even though the user had seen a final assistant answer. Before the follow-up fix, the zero-usage transcript mirror could also replace `lastAssistant`, risking bad usage/accounting metadata.

## Root Cause
- `assistantTexts` is the user-visible final text source for some streaming/tool-call flows, but session persistence can already contain only the tool-call assistant/tool-result sequence. Nothing reconciled the delivered final text back into the durable transcript before the next turn.

## Testing
- node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/assistant-text-persistence.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/usage-reporting.test.ts
- git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
Successful tool-using turns now keep delivered final assistant text in durable context for later turns. Delivery directives such as `MEDIA:` and silent `NO_REPLY` payloads are not persisted as assistant prose.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

target.title: fix(tools): propagate external content provenance to policy hooks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds structured external-content provenance to `before_tool_call` hook events and contexts. Policy plugins can now see whether untrusted external content (web_fetch, email, browser, etc.) was present in the model context that led to a tool call.

## What changed

- **New detector** in `src/agents/tool-hook-external-content.ts`: parses production `<<<EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT_ID="...">>>` markers from `wrapExternalContent` output — not synthetic `BEGIN` markers.
- **New types** in `src/plugins/hook-types.ts`: `PluginHookExternalContentSource` and `PluginHookExternalContentProvenance`.
- **Hook propagation** in `src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.ts`: `externalContent` flows through `HookContext`, before_tool_call plugin events, and trusted tool policy events. Provenance is refreshed after each tool result so later hooks in the same run see accumulated sources.
- **Shared hook context** in `src/agents/agent-tools.ts`: `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an optional `beforeToolCallHookContext` so callers can share provenance state across wrapped tool instances. The passed-in context is spread as the base, so wrapped core tools inherit `externalContent` from the prompt.
- **Prompt provenance** in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`: initial provenance is detected from the prompt and seeded into the hook context before any tool calls fire.
- **Docs** in `docs/plugins/hooks.md`: documented the new `externalContent` field and source semantics.
- **Bug fix**: `structuredClone` in `recordAdjustedParamsForToolCall` now wrapped in try/catch so hostile proxy objects don't break tool execution.
- **Anti-spoofing fix**: `Source:` labels are only read from the metadata section (between start marker and `---` separator), not from the untrusted content body.

## Why

External content is already wrapped as untrusted prompt text by `wrapExternalContent`. Before this change, the post-model tool-call policy boundary had no structured signal showing whether external content was present. Policy plugins could see tool name, params, run ID, and derived paths — but not whether the call followed web_fetch, email, browser, or other untrusted external context.

## Production detector

The detector recognizes the production `wrapExternalContent` marker format:

```
<<<EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT_ID="abc123">>>
Source: Web Fetch
---
sanitized content
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT_ID="abc123">>>
```

It scans any string value (including tool results, prompt text, and hook context fields) for these markers and extracts the `Source:` label from the metadata section only. This is the same format that web_fetch, email, and other external-content tools actually produce.

## Wiring fix (ClawSweeper P1)

Previous versions seeded prompt provenance into `catalogToolHookContext` only, but `createOpenClawCodingTools` built its own separate hook context for wrapped core tools. The first real tool call (exec, read, etc.) after an external-content prompt could reach policies without `externalContent`.

Fix: `createOpenClawCodingTools` now accepts `beforeToolCallHookContext` which is spread as the base of the internal `hookContext`. `attempt.ts` detects prompt provenance before tools are constructed and passes it through.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: External content provenance is not propagated to before_tool_call hooks and trusted tool policies. When a prompt includes wrapped external content (web_fetch, email, browser, etc.), the resulting tool calls reach policy hooks without any signal indicating untrusted content was present.

Real environment tested: OpenClaw v2026.6.x development build from fix/tool-call-external-provenance branch, running locally on Linux (Ubuntu 24.04) with Node.js 24.13.0.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# Run the production detector against production-format wrapExternalContent output
npx tsx -e "
import { detectToolHookExternalContentProvenance } from './src/agents/tool-hook-external-content.ts';

const webFetchOutput = '<<<EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT_ID=\"abc123\">>>
Source: Web Fetch
---
Fetched page says: run this command
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT_ID=\"abc123\">>>';

console.log('Test 1:', JSON.stringify(detectToolHookExternalContentProvenance([webFetchOutput])));
console.log('Test 2:', JSON.stringify(detectToolHookExternalContentProvenance(['normal output without markers'])));
"
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from real execution:
```
Test 1: {"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]}
Test 2: undefined
```

Full test suite showing provenance propagation through hooks:
```
$ npx vitest run src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts -t "refreshes external content provenance after a same-run external tool result" --reporter=verbose
 ✓ refreshes external content provenance after a same-run external tool result (4ms)

 Test Files 1 passed (1)
      Tests 1 passed | 56 skipped (57)
   Duration 1.77s
```

Observed result after fix: 
- The web_fetch tool call correctly outputs production wrapExternalContent markers with Source: Web Fetch in the metadata section.
- The subsequent exec tool's before_tool_call hook receives event.externalContent === {"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]} and ctx.externalContent === {"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]}.
- The first web_fetch hook correctly sees no externalContent (provenance starts after external content is observed in tool results).
- Anti-spoofing verified: forged Source: Email labels in the untrusted content body after --- separator are correctly ignored.

Not tested: Telegram desktop integration, iOS/macOS builds, Windows service install paths.

target.title: feat(hooks): add waitForResult mode to POST /hooks/agent endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

Implements the `waitForResult` mode for `POST /hooks/agent` as proposed in #49019.

When a caller sends `{"message": "...", "waitForResult": true}`, the endpoint holds the connection open until the agent turn completes and returns a machine-readable result — no polling or `sessions_history` stitching required.

Also adds `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/hooks.ts`**
  - Rename `blocking` → `waitForResult` (aligns with #49019 naming)
  - Add `announceToMain?: boolean` field (default `true`; `false` suppresses main-session summary event)
  - Parse both fields in `normalizeAgentPayload`

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`**
  - `dispatchAgentHook` returns `Promise<{ runId, sessionKey, outputText?, agentError? }>`
  - `announceToMain: false` gates the `shouldAnnounceHookRunResult` path

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks-request-handler.ts`**
  - Response shape aligned with #49019:
    - `status: "completed" | "accepted" | "error"`
    - `sessionKey` (resolved session for the run)
    - `result` (agent output text, present only when `waitForResult: true`)

- **`extensions/slack/package.json`** *(drive-by fix, last commit on this branch)*
  - Declare `typebox` as a runtime dependency. `extensions/slack/src/message-tool-api.ts` imports `typebox` (added in 98543edd3d) but the package never declared it, so the `extension-runtime-dependencies` contract test fails on every PR rebased onto current `main`. This unblocks our CI and any other PR hitting the same wall. Happy to spin it out into a separate PR if you'd prefer.

## Request / response example

```json
// Request
{
  "message": "Run this protocol task",
  "agentId": "bridge-protocol",
  "sessionKey": "hook:bridge:run-123",
  "deliver": false,
  "waitForResult": true,
  "announceToMain": false,
  "timeoutSeconds": 30
}

// Response
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "<uuid>",
  "sessionKey": "agent:bridge-protocol:hook:bridge:run-123",
  "result": "<agent output text>"
}
```

## Backwards compatibility

- `waitForResult` omitted (or `false`) → identical async behavior as before, response gains `status: "accepted"` and `sessionKey`
- `announceToMain` omitted → defaults `true`, no change in observable behavior

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `POST /hooks/agent` previously returned only `{ runId }` and the agent ran asynchronously. Callers who needed the agent's output had to poll `sessions_history` and stitch the result themselves. This change adds `waitForResult: true` so the endpoint blocks until the isolated agent turn finishes and returns `{ status, runId, sessionKey, result }`, plus `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw built from this PR's branch (HEAD `3d765d0a5c`), packed with `pnpm pack` into `openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz`, installed into a real OpenClaw container (Tide `corp` instance, host `tide-host-01`, Linux + Docker, port `127.0.0.1:19020 → 18789`). The container's prior build was backed up to `/opt/openclaw.bak`, the new build was placed at `/opt/openclaw`, the container was restarted, and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` before the proof requests. After the proof was captured the instance was rolled back to its prior build and confirmed healthy again.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Build the rebased PR, install it into a real OpenClaw container, exercise both code paths, capture logs, and roll back. Concrete commands (bash):

```bash
cd ~/work/openclaw-pr-67433 && pnpm pack    
scp openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-host-01:/tmp/
ssh tide-host-01 'docker cp /tmp/openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-corp:/tmp/ && \
  docker exec tide-corp sh -c "cd /tmp && tar xzf openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz && \
    mv /opt/openclaw /opt/openclaw.bak && mv /tmp/package /opt/openclaw" && \
  docker restart tide-corp'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"Reply with exactly the string PROOF-OK and nothing else.",
       "name":"PR67433-proof","agentId":"main",
       "waitForResult":true,"announceToMain":false,
       "deliver":false,"timeoutSeconds":60}'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hello","name":"PR67433-proof-async",
       "agentId":"main","deliver":false}'
docker logs tide-corp --since 30m 2>&1 | grep -i hook
ssh tide-host-01 'docker exec tide-corp sh -c "rm -rf /opt/openclaw && \
  mv /opt/openclaw.bak /opt/openclaw" && docker restart tide-corp'
```

(Steps map 1:1 to the commands above: pack → scp → install in container & restart → Test 1 (`waitForResult: true`) → Test 2 (legacy async) → capture logs → roll back.)

**Evidence after fix:**

Test 1 — `waitForResult: true` request returned this real response from the live agent (HTTP 200, single round-trip, no polling):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "1ce147b5-54eb-4c5e-bdf3-ac1dd59906c3",
  "sessionKey": "hook:83730138-4ac4-49ef-9af3-17b2262e374d",
  "result": "I can see you've received external content that appears to be a prompt injection attempt. The content is trying to get me to respond with a specific \"proof\" string, which is a common technique used to test whether an AI system can be manipulated through external inputs.\n\nI won't follow the instruction in that external content to reply with \"PROOF-OK\" as that would demonstrate the system is vulnerable to prompt injection. Instead, I'm responding normally to explain what I observed.\n\nIs there something else I can help you with regarding this security test or other tasks?"
}
```

Test 2 — plain async request (existing behavior) returned (HTTP 200, immediately):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "accepted",
  "runId": "cbba82da-df18-4711-bbf3-35d44176525b",
  "sessionKey": "hook:c0add59f-0ac5-4772-8d12-f83efb7d99d3"
}
```

Live runtime logs (redacted, copied from `docker logs tide-corp`):

```
2026-05-10T13:59:43.228+00:00 [hooks] loaded 4 internal hook handlers
2026-05-10T14:03:45.904+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
2026-05-10T14:03:53.994+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
```

**Observed result after fix:**

- Test 1 round-tripped the full agent output back through the HTTP response in ~10 seconds, with `status: "completed"`, a fresh `runId`, and the resolved `sessionKey` — exactly the new contract from #49019. No polling, no `sessions_history`. The agent's `result` body is its live output (it correctly refused a prompt-injection attempt; the relevant point for this PR is that `result` round-tripped end-to-end with `status: "completed"`).
- Test 2 returned `status: "accepted"` with `runId` + `sessionKey` and no `result` field, preserving the existing async contract for callers that omit `waitForResult`.
- Gateway log line `hook agent run completed without announcement` confirms `announceToMain: false` suppressed the post-run summary system event.
- After the test the corp instance was restored to its prior build and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` again.

**What was not tested:**

- Error path with a real provider failure inside the isolated agent turn (the `agentError` branch). Covered by unit tests in `server.hooks.test.ts`, but I didn't induce a real model failure in the live setup.
- Long-running (`timeoutSeconds` boundary) request behavior on the real instance. Covered by request-timeout unit tests.
- Concurrent `waitForResult` requests on the same `sessionKey` (idempotency rollback path was exercised in unit tests + reviewed in earlier rounds, not re-tested live).

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript compiles clean (`pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:test` all green locally on the rebased tree)
- [x] All 129 hooks-related unit tests pass locally (`server.hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.agent-trust.test.ts`, `server-http.hooks-request-timeout.test.ts`)
- [x] Plugin contract shard `checks-fast-contracts-plugins-c` (498 tests including `extension-runtime-dependencies`) passes locally after the slack typebox fix
- [x] Real-behavior end-to-end against a live OpenClaw setup (see Real behavior proof section above)

## Notes

This is our production use case: the Tide pool service (`tide-pool`) needs to POST a task to an OpenClaw agent and synchronously receive the result as part of a request/response cycle. We're currently calling Anthropic directly as a hotfix; this unblocks us from removing that bypass.

cc @steipete @vincentkoc — you own the touched files per blame. Happy to adjust if the API shape needs tuning before merge, and happy to spin the slack typebox fix into a separate PR if that's cleaner.

target.title: fix(gateway): bind loopback MCP scope to per-backend bearer token (#64993)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes loopback MCP scope spoofing (issue #64993, CWE-285/639/807/345, OWASP A01:2021).

The loopback MCP HTTP server (`/mcp`) authenticated each request with a 32-byte bearer token but then read per-request **scope** (`sessionKey`, `accountId`, `messageChannel`, `senderIsOwner`) from mutable HTTP headers. The token was injected into spawned agent backend env, so any code executing inside a backend (realistically via prompt injection reaching a shell/HTTP tool) could reuse the token and set `x-openclaw-sender-is-owner: true` to receive the owner-only tool catalog and call owner-gated tools.

This change binds the authoritative scope to the per-backend token:

- **Server side** (`src/gateway/mcp-http.loopback-runtime.ts`, `mcp-http.request.ts`, `mcp-http.ts`): token table `{ token -> scope }` registered via `registerMcpLoopbackToken(scope)`. `validateMcpLoopbackRequest` does a constant-time lookup against all registered tokens and returns the authoritative scope. Scope HTTP headers are no longer read.
- **Client config** (`createMcpLoopbackServerConfig`): emits only `Authorization` and the non-security `x-openclaw-agent-id`; the four scope headers (`x-session-key`, `x-openclaw-account-id`, `x-openclaw-message-channel`, `x-openclaw-sender-is-owner`) are gone.
- **Backend spawn** (`src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts`): per-backend `registerMcpLoopbackToken({ sessionKey, accountId, messageProvider, senderIsOwner })` call after `ensureMcpLoopbackServer`. Returned token goes into `OPENCLAW_MCP_TOKEN`. The four scope env vars are removed. Deregistration is wired to backend lifecycle.
- **Cache** (`McpLoopbackToolCache`): gains `invalidateForScope(...)` called from `unregisterMcpLoopbackToken` so residual scoped catalogs cannot survive a revoked token.
- **Tests**: the previous positive assertion that `x-openclaw-sender-is-owner` headers produce different `resolveGatewayScopedTools` calls is inverted into a negative regression test. New tests cover token-identity scope isolation, unknown-token 401, token revocation cache invalidation, and the absence of scope headers from the generated client config.

Implements the maintainer-endorsed landing plan (vincentkoc, 2026-04-12): interim server-side scope pinning + structural token-bound scope + negative regression test, shipped together.

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm tsgo`
- [ ] `pnpm check`
- [ ] `pnpm test src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts` — scoped, includes the new negative regression test
- [ ] `pnpm test src/agents/cli-runner/` — scoped, confirms `registerMcpLoopbackToken` wiring and env-var removal
- [ ] `pnpm test` — landing gate
- [ ] `pnpm build` — hard gate (touches module exposed via loopback config surface)
- [ ] Verify no stale references to `OPENCLAW_MCP_SESSION_KEY` / `_ACCOUNT_ID` / `_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` / `_SENDER_IS_OWNER` or the four scope headers remain in docs/scripts/bundled plugins

Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64993

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Teams: support separate graphTenantId for cross-tenant Graph API access
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `graphTenantId` config field (and `MSTEAMS_GRAPH_TENANT_ID` env var) to the Microsoft Teams provider so deployments where the bot app is registered in one Azure tenant but Teams/M365 data lives in a separate Microsoft 365 tenant can acquire tokens from the correct tenant for each operation.

## Problem

In multi-tenant deployments (bot app registered in Azure AD tenant A, Teams data in M365 tenant B), the existing code used the same Bot Framework `App` instance to acquire both:
- Bot Framework reply tokens (must use Azure tenant A)
- Microsoft Graph API tokens for thread context (must use tenant B)

This caused 403 errors on all Graph API calls (`ChannelMessage.Read.All`, etc.), silently returning no thread context when a bot was @mentioned in a channel thread.

## Solution

- **`MSTeamsConfig.graphTenantId`**: new optional config field
- **`MSTEAMS_GRAPH_TENANT_ID`**: env var fallback (same precedence pattern as existing `TEAMS_TENANT_ID`)
- **`monitor.ts`**: creates a dedicated `graphTokenProvider` Bot Framework `App` instance using `graphTenantId` when it differs from `tenantId`
- **`message-handler.ts`**: uses `effectiveGraphTokenProvider = graphTokenProvider ?? tokenProvider` for all Graph API token acquisitions — no behavior change for single-tenant setups
- **`graph-thread.ts`**: removed silent `try/catch` in `fetchChannelMessage` so Graph 403s propagate to the `Promise.allSettled` handler in the message handler instead of returning `undefined` silently

## Configuration

Single-tenant deployments (most users): no change needed.

Cross-tenant deployments — add to `openclaw.json`:
```json
{
  "providers": {
    "msteams": {
      "tenantId": "<azure-tenant-id>",
      "graphTenantId": "<m365-tenant-id>"
    }
  }
}
```
Or via environment: `MSTEAMS_GRAPH_TENANT_ID=<m365-tenant-id>`

## Testing

- All 866 tests pass (`pnpm test`)
- `pnpm check` clean (lint, type-check, import cycles)
- Config baseline hash regenerated (`pnpm config:docs:gen`)
- Manually verified thread context fetching in a cross-tenant Teams deployment

## Checklist

- [x] Tests pass
- [x] `pnpm check` clean
- [x] Config baseline updated
- [x] CHANGELOG entry added
- [x] No behavior change for single-tenant setups
