target.title: External reranker
target.body:
## Summary What problem does this PR solve? OpenClaw's memory-core has no support for external rerankers whe using memorySearch:query:hybrid — only the built-in MMR algorithm or switching the entire system to QMD. QMD is local-only, so the reranker runs on the gateway. This change allows users to configure an external reranker with memory-core. Why does this matter now? It increases the quality of results from memory searches. As internal memories grow, ensuring the best results becomes more important. What is the intended outcome? Better results from memory searches. What is intentionally out of scope? QMD, LanceDB, and other memory systems for Openclaw. What does success look like? Subtle, but important, the user doesn't notice any difference other than "Openclaw is better at finding information in memories". What should reviewers focus on? Look at the interface used between memory-core and MMR. MMR has been moved to a plugin, and the new external reranker uses the same interface.
Summary guidance This PR makes two changes. First, it moves mmr to a bundled plugin called memory-mmr. Second, it adds a memory-external-reranker plugin to memory-core, enabling external reranker support for Cohere-compatible /v1/rerank endpoints. It is designed so that users with existing memorySearch:query:hybrid configuruations see no difference and don't have to update their configuration to continue to use the existing mmr. Only when the user enables and memory-external-reranker plugin and adds the other necessary config entries will the external reranker be used. it's also designed so the user can configure mmr as a fallback if the external reranker fails for any reason (plugin not loaded, external reranker call fails).
## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes #64438 Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? Related # Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
Linked context guidance https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64438
## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: external reranker called during memory recall - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Logs from a running OpenClaw instance showing the external reranker in action: ``` 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] reranking with provider=local-llm-server-02-8082 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 fallbacks=none topN=30 documents=34 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 url=http://local-llm-server-02:8082/v1/rerank topN=30 documents=34 ``` ... ``` 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 response status=200 ok=true 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 success: 30 documents reranked ``` The reranker plugin loaded as a gateway plugin, received 34 candidate documents, sent them to the remote endpoint, and returned 30 reranked results. - Observed result after fix: better search results from memory - What was not tested: QMD, LanceDB, other memory systems - Proof limitations or environment constraints: - Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
Real behavior proof guidance config json fragments agents:defaults: ``` "memorySearch": { "enabled": true, "provider": "openai-compatible", "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "query": { "hybrid": { "enabled": true, "vectorWeight": 0.7, "textWeight": 0.3, "mmr": { "enabled": true, "lambda": 0.7, "provider": "memory-external-reranker", "fallback": "memory-mmr" } } }, "remote": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8081/v1", "apiKey": { "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "LOCAL_LLM_02_KEY" }, "batch": { "enabled": false }, "nonBatchConcurrency": 3 }, } ``` models:providers: ``` "local-llm-server-02-8082": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8082", "models": [ { "id": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "name": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "input": [ "text" ] } ] } ``` plugins:entries: ``` "memory-external-reranker": { "enabled": true, "config": { "provider": "local-llm-server-02-8082", "model": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "topN": 30 } }, ``` - Real environment tested: multiple test VMs. I've been running it on my production Openclaw instance for over a week. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enable and configure the memory-external-reranker plugin, index memory, perform a memory search like "What do you remember about the Lucid Air?" See attached screenshot.
## Tests and validation Which commands did you run? pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test pnpm test:extension memory-external-reranker pnpm test:extension memory-mmr What regression coverage was added or updated? Full tests added for both MMR plugin and new external reranker plugin. What failed before this fix, if known? If no test was added, why not?
Testing guidance To do full testing, an embedding model and reranking model are needed. I use llamacpp on localhost and update openclaw.json to point to the local models. ``` ./llama-server -hf Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF --port 8081 --embedding ./llama-server -hf ggml-org/Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B-Q8_0-GGUF --port 8082 --embedding --reranking --pooling rank ```
## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? No Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Yes. New config entries added Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No. Uses existing patterns and code to call external reranker. What is the highest-risk area? Memory search results are not reranked by either the external reranker nor MMR. How is that risk mitigated? MMR is a bundled plugin. As long as mmr is a buldled plugin and loaded by default, the risk is minimal.
Risk guidance Minimal risk once MMR is a bundled plugin.
## Current review state What is the next action? What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
Review state guidance Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

target.title: fix: prevent memory flush failure from aborting user reply (#85645)
target.body:
## Summary

When `memoryFlush` fails during the reply pipeline (provider timeout, rate limit, network error), the visible error payload was treated as the final reply via `replyOperation.fail("run_failed", ...)`. This aborted the user's actual message. Since the session remained over threshold, every subsequent message triggered the same flush failure, creating a permanent death loop where no real replies could be delivered.

## Changes

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`**
- Memory flush visible error payloads are now logged via `logVerbose()` and **not** delivered as the reply. The main reply run proceeds normally.
- On flush exhaustion (`memoryFlushFailureCount` crosses `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES` during the current turn), the bloated session is automatically rotated via `resetReplyRunSession()`. The old transcript file is **preserved on disk** (`cleanupTranscripts: false`) — rotation alone breaks the death loop (the new session no longer hits the bloated transcript), and keeping the old JSONL leaves it available for forensics/recovery. The active session only replays recent turns regardless of this flag.
- Added an **opt-in, non-terminal degraded notice**: when `agents.defaults.compaction.notifyUser` is enabled, flush exhaustion surfaces a short heads-up to the user via the existing `onBlockReply` bypass (same mechanism as `sendCompactionNotice`). It **never** calls `replyOperation.fail()` and **never** aborts the reply. Default (`notifyUser=false`) keeps the silent log-and-continue behavior. No new config surface is added.
- The `resetSession` / `resetSessionAfterRoleOrderingConflict` helpers are hoisted earlier in the function so they are available before the memory flush phase. No behavioral change to their existing callers; the role-ordering reset path keeps its original `cleanupTranscripts: true`.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`**
- `initSessionState()` now clears `memoryFlushFailureCount`, `memoryFlushLastFailedAt`, and `memoryFlushLastFailureError` when a session is reset (e.g., via `/new`), preventing stale failure state from carrying over.

**`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.ts`**
- `resetReplyRunSession()` now clears `compactionCount` (reset to 0) and all `memoryFlush*` fields, ensuring a cleanly rotated session has no lingering flush state.

## What is NOT changed

- `runMemoryFlushIfNeeded` behavior is unchanged — it still tracks failures, increments `memoryFlushFailureCount`, and emits lifecycle events (`memory_flush_failed`, `memory_flush_exhausted`).
- `fallbacksOverride: []` is preserved — flush operations still use a fixed model/provider with no fallback chain.
- `MAX_FLUSH_FAILURES = 3` threshold is unchanged.
- No new configuration key is introduced — the opt-in notice reuses the existing `compaction.notifyUser` gate.

## Tests

- **`agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts`**: Verifies flush errors are logged and the main reply continues; verifies session rotation on flush exhaustion now uses `cleanupTranscripts: false`; covers the opt-in notice (fires on exhaustion when `notifyUser=true`, stays silent on transient failure, and never fires when `notifyUser=false`) — in all cases the main reply still proceeds.
- **`agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts`**: Verifies `compactionCount` and `memoryFlush*` fields are cleared on reset; adds a test proving that with `cleanupTranscripts: false` the session still rotates to a new id while the old transcript file is **preserved** on disk.
- **`session.test.ts`**: Verifies `initSessionState()` clears all memory flush state fields, with both in-memory assertions and persisted session store file reads.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When `memoryFlush` / auto-compaction fails permanently (provider timeout, rate limit, 5xx), current `main` routes the visible flush error into `replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` before the main reply run, aborting the user's real reply. Because the session stays over threshold, every later message hits the same failure — a permanent death loop with no real replies delivered. This PR logs the flush failure and continues the main reply, rotates the exhausted session while preserving the old transcript on disk (`cleanupTranscripts: false`), clears stale flush state on reset, and adds an opt-in non-terminal degraded notice gated by the existing `compaction.notifyUser`.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw Gateway run directly from this PR's source at current head `5dc502d76f` (branch `fix/memory-flush-death-loop-85645`) via `tsx` (`node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway run`), on macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Isolated state dir, `auth: none`, Gateway port `14881`. A mock OpenAI-completions provider on loopback `:3999` returns HTTP `500` for compaction/flush requests (detected by summarization-prompt keywords) and HTTP `200` for normal chat — isolating the flush-failure path while the main reply provider stays healthy. Config: `softThresholdTokens: 800`, `contextWindow: 4096`, `maxTokens: 1024`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Start the mock provider: `node mock-provider/server.mjs` (loopback `:3999`, returns 500 on flush requests, 200 on normal chat).
  2. Start the Gateway from this PR's source at head `5dc502d76f`: `node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway --force --port 14881 --auth none run` with an isolated state dir.
  3. Drive 12 user messages over the WebSocket protocol: 6 to grow the session past the 800-token soft threshold, then 6 more to repeatedly trigger the (permanently failing) flush. Wait for each `agent.wait` completion.
- **Before evidence (current main)**: On current `main`, visible memory-flush error payloads are collected and passed to `replyOperation.fail("run_failed")` before the main reply run, so the user's real reply is aborted and subsequent messages repeat the failure (`src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` flush-error path on `main`).
- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs and terminal console output copied from a live OpenClaw Gateway run (`node --import tsx src/index.ts gateway --force --port 14881 --auth none run`) at head `5dc502d76f`, with private data redacted.

  Mock provider — every flush request rejected with 500 (36 flush 500s, 1 normal 200):
  ```
  [req#1]  NORMAL REQUEST — returning success
  [req#2]  FLUSH REQUEST DETECTED — returning 500
  [req#3]  FLUSH REQUEST DETECTED — returning 500
  ...
  (36 flush requests total, all returned HTTP 500)
  ```

  Gateway — flush failures logged, session mapping preserved, run continues (not aborted):
  ```
  [agent/embedded] auto-compaction failed for mock/mock-model:
    Summarization failed: 500 Mock provider: simulated flush failure
  [agent/embedded] [context-overflow-recovery] exhausted provider overflow recovery
    for mock/mock-model; livenessState=blocked suggestedAction=reset_or_new
  Auto-compaction failed (...). Preserving existing session mapping for [SESSION].
  ```

  WebSocket client — all 12 messages received a reply (`status=ok`), none aborted:
  ```
  [MSG-1]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-2]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-3]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-4]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-5]      agent done: status=ok
  [MSG-6]      agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-7]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-8]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-9]  agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-10] agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-11] agent done: status=ok
  [TRIGGER-12] agent done: status=ok
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the flush provider permanently broken (36 consecutive 500s), all 12 user messages still completed with `status=ok` — the death loop is gone. The flush failure is logged rather than delivered as a terminal `run_failed` reply, and the session mapping is preserved/rotated instead of aborting the turn. This is the exact condition that previously produced the permanent loop on `main`.
- **What was not tested**: Live third-party LLM providers (the flush failure is simulated with a deterministic mock 500 to make the path reproducible); long-horizon multi-session persistence across Gateway restarts; and the maintainer-judgment items (session-rotation liveness tradeoff and the opt-in notice semantics) which are product decisions rather than testable code behavior.

target.title: feat(claude): add claude-bridge app-server harness extension
target.body:
## Summary

TL;DR:
Inspiration: https://openclaw.ai/blog/openai-models-in-openclaw-done-right; goal: achieve parity for Claude

New feature; issue https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/93793

OpenClaw currently has no first-class harness for Anthropic models. Anthropic users either fall back to a generic provider client (no native tool execution, no extended-thinking awareness, no per-turn rate-limit surfacing) or run a Claude harness out-of-tree with no contract for OpenClaw's plugin-SDK capabilities and features.

The codex harness already ships in-tree with the same shape we want — `extensions/codex/src/app-server/` is a codex-app-server JSON-RPC client over stdio. We have a working Anthropic-side equivalent built and running in production on the author's setup; opening it for maintainer steer before doing more SDK work on top.

Harness: this PR
JSON-RPC server: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge

**What is the intended outcome?**

- Bundled extension `@openclaw/claude` registers `claude-bridge` as a harness option in the model picker.
- At turn-start, the extension spawns an `openclaw-claude-bridge` subprocess and speaks the codex-shaped JSON-RPC protocol over stdio, directly wrapping the latest claude agent SDK. The server (currently published as `@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge`  https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge — fork-preview path; would migrate to `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` if the maintainers republish it) handles Anthropic API I/O, rate-limit surfacing, image-payload sanitization, and plugin-thread-config persistence.
- Bridge-side handles event projection into OpenClaw's notification/accumulator surface, transcript mirroring with idempotency keys, dynamic-tool aggregate output cap, vision-tools de-duplication, and `/claude` slash commands.
- See `extensions/claude/README.md` for the full feature map.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**

- Republishing the server under `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` — needs maintainer decision; the `@zeroaltitude` scope dependency is the current placeholder.
- Migrating to OpenClaw's standard plugin-SDK provider hooks beyond what `extensions/codex/` already uses. The bridge follows codex's pattern verbatim where applicable.
- Anything outside the harness bridge surface.

**What does success look like?** Anthropic users get parity with codex users: native tool dispatch, rate-limit-aware errors, extended-thinking-friendly transcript mirroring, harness-level event projection. No regressions to existing harnesses.

**What should reviewers focus on?**

1. **Scope concern — am I taking too much core?** The branch touches `src/plugins/command-registration.ts`, `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.ts`, `src/plugins/agent-tool-result-middleware-types.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-native-slash-fast-path.ts`, `src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.ts`, and `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`. Each is small and necessary to wire a harness extension, but the contribution guide says "one thing per PR." Happy to split into prep PRs first if maintainers prefer.
2. **External-plugin vs. bundled-extension** — Per CONTRIBUTING.md "most features should be third-party plugins." Open to packaging this entirely as an external plugin instead of `extensions/claude/`, if that is preferred.
3. **Anthropic-API behavior delegated to a separate server repo** — Most of the actual API logic lives in the server (https://github.com/zeroaltitude/openclaw-plugins/tree/main/openclaw-claude/server -- https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge). Reviewers will probably want to inspect that companion repo before approving.
4. **`claude-bridge` harness id naming** — there's no precedent for "<vendor>-bridge" harness names; renamable. (The reason I did this initially was to ensure that I wasn't representing that this work was by anthropic -- matters more on the server naming than the in-openclaw extension tho)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Anthropic-model agents previously had no in-tree harness; the bridge unlocks native tool runs against Claude through the same JSON-RPC pattern codex uses.
- **Real environment tested:** Author's openclaw gateway running on Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu), Node 22.x. Author's agents  are configured with `claude-bridge` as their harness and are in daily active use against `claude-sonnet-4-6` and `claude-opus-4-7`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `pnpm install && pnpm build`
  2. `openclaw gateway restart --deep`
  3. Send a Slack DM to one of the claude-bridge agents
  4. Agent responds with native Anthropic tools used; transcript file contains the projected events.
  5. `/claude status` in the same thread prints shared-client liveness.
- **Evidence after fix (fresh capture, 2026-05-28):** This gateway runs the claude-bridge harness as the default runtime for all Anthropic models (`agents.defaults.models.*.agentRuntime.id = "claude-bridge"`), so the harness is exercised continuously across multiple agents. Captured live:
  1. **Bridge liveness** — shared-client lifecycle today: `claude-bridge: spawned ×5 / initialized ×5 / process exited ×5` (restarts and re-initializes without manual intervention).
  2. **A turn completing with visible tool/progress output** — one bridge turn dispatching consecutive tools, each completing, with per-tool provenance timing from the observability plugin: `ToolSearch → after_tool_call 1.9ms`, `exec → 1.7ms`, `Write → 1.9ms`, `Agent → 1.9ms`.
  3. **Transcript / result event stream** — the run persists a projected event stream to `~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/sessions/<uuid>.jsonl`: parentId-chained `message` events covering the user turn, assistant `tool_use` blocks, and `tool_result` blocks.
  4. **`/claude status`** (live, from a bridge-backed agent):
     ```
     Claude app-server status
     Shared client: running
     Command: openclaw-claude-bridge
     Last stderr: [turn-runner] system event {"subtype":"init"} / {"subtype":"status"} / unhandled SDK event {"type":"rate_limit_event"}
     ```
  *Log-hygiene note:* a few `claude-bridge: runAttempt failed` lines appear in the same logs; they are not bridge defects — one class is a context-overflow on a zero-message scheduled run (auto-compaction correctly skipped), the other is transient model-call failover during an MCP reconnect; both recovered on the next turn. Earlier daily-driver context still applies: Tabitha (claude-bridge) has been in active production use for ~2 weeks.
- **Observed result after fix:** Anthropic turns complete normally; tool calls fire; rate-limit messages are user-actionable; resume across restart works via the `.claude-binding.json` sidecar.
- **What was not tested:**
  - Windows/macOS gateway hosts (only Linux/WSL2 verified).
  - Bun runtime (Node 22 only).
  - Anthropic enterprise/Bedrock/Vertex API surfaces (only public Anthropic API).
  - Concurrent multi-account scenarios beyond 3 agents.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Author runs a single-host gateway. The server is currently published under the author's `@zeroaltitude` npm scope (fork-preview), so reproducing this requires installing the bridge from that scope until maintainers republish.
- **Before evidence:** N/A — no prior in-tree harness for Anthropic to compare against.

## Tests and validation

- Real world use for over a month on a broad diversity of tasks with observed, anecdotal, improvements in coding, token usage and well executed long running tasks
- `pnpm build` ✓ (local)
- `pnpm test extensions/claude` ✓ (local — extension unit tests, including event projector, dynamic-tools middleware port, thread fork policy envelope, transcript mirror dedupe)
- `pnpm test:extensions` not run since last rebase — recommend Testbox/Crabbox run before non-draft review.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit per contributor rule; maintainer to add on land.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — new `claude-bridge` harness appears in model picker; new `/claude` slash command surface.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** Yes — new plugin `configSchema` (see `extensions/claude/openclaw.plugin.json`). Existing agent configs unaffected unless they explicitly opt into `claude-bridge` harness. Doctor contract added (`extensions/claude/doctor-contract-api.ts`).
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** Yes (intentional). The bridge spawns a subprocess that holds the Anthropic API key in-memory and makes network calls to Anthropic. Subprocess lifecycle is owned by the harness, not user shell. The plugin SDK enabler changes (e.g. `command-registration.ts`) are additive and follow existing codex-extension precedent.
- **Highest-risk area:** Server-bridge stdio lifecycle and the few SDK enabler edits in `src/plugins/**`. If the SDK changes accidentally relax a guard for non-claude paths, every harness would inherit the change.
- **How is that risk mitigated?** The SDK changes are narrowly scoped — each follows a codex-extension-shaped precedent. Happy to factor them into a prep PR if reviewers want to land them separately first.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer steer on scope: (a) accept as bundled extension as-is, (b) split SDK enablers into a prep PR first, or (c) request packaging as an external plugin.
- **Still waiting on:** Maintainer decision on the above. CI run on this push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** None yet — opening draft.

---

🤖 **AI-assisted disclosure:** Developed with the assistance of Claude Code (anthropic.com/claude-code). All design decisions, production deployment, and real-behavior verification are human-driven; AI assistance was used for implementation across multiple sessions. target.title: fix(proxy): redact sensitive headers in standalone debug proxy captures
target.body:
Fixes #90009

## Problem

The standalone debug proxy (`openclaw proxy start`) stores raw request and response headers in capture events via `JSON.stringify(req.headers)` without redaction. This includes `Authorization`, `x-api-key`, `Cookie`, and other sensitive headers in the capture database.

The runtime proxy path (`runtime.ts`) already uses `redactedCaptureHeaders()` with a comprehensive sensitive-header allowlist (lines 19-41), but the standalone `proxy-server.ts` does not. This inconsistency means captured traffic from `openclaw proxy start` contains secrets that `openclaw proxy run` would redact.

## Fix

Add a `redactHeaders` function to `proxy-server.ts` that delegates sensitivity
detection to the **shared** `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` function exported from
`runtime.ts`. This eliminates the duplicate header lists and ensures the
standalone proxy and the in-process fetch-patch always use the identical
sensitivity logic (exact names + fragment matching). The function is applied to
all three header storage points:
1. HTTP request headers (outbound capture)
2. HTTP response headers (inbound capture)
3. CONNECT request headers (tunnel capture)

**Centralization**: `proxy-server.ts` imports `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` from
`runtime.ts` (lines 20–41 there) rather than duplicating the constant lists.
Changes to the sensitive-header vocabulary are now single-source.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Debug proxy capture events stored raw `Authorization`, `Cookie`, `X-Api-Key`, and other sensitive headers in the capture SQLite database. The standalone proxy path (`openclaw proxy start`) was missing the redaction that the in-process fetch-patch already applies.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 92bc1d4) at `/tmp/fix-82951`. Debug proxy started via `node openclaw.mjs proxy start --port 18999`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82951
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Start the debug proxy
node openclaw.mjs proxy start --port 18999

Step 3. Send a request with sensitive headers through the proxy
curl -x http://127.0.0.1:18999 \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-SUPER-SECRET-API-KEY-12345" \
  -H "Cookie: session_id=PRIVATE-SESSION-abc123" \
  -H "X-Api-Key: xai-SECRET-KEY-67890" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "User-Agent: openclaw-proof/1.0" \
  http://httpbin.org/headers

Step 4. Query the capture database to verify redaction
sqlite3 ~/.openclaw/debug-proxy/capture.sqlite \
  "SELECT direction, method, host, headers_json FROM capture_events
   ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 2;"
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Live debug proxy redaction proof**

Sent a real HTTP request with 3 sensitive headers (`Authorization`, `Cookie`, `X-Api-Key`) through the patched debug proxy to httpbin.org. Then queried the capture database:

**Outbound request capture (what the proxy stored):**
```console
$ sqlite3 ~/.openclaw/debug-proxy/capture.sqlite \
    "SELECT headers_json FROM capture_events WHERE direction='outbound' ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 1;"
{"host":"httpbin.org","accept":"*/*","proxy-connection":"Keep-Alive","authorization":"[REDACTED]","cookie":"[REDACTED]","x-api-key":"[REDACTED]","content-type":"application/json","user-agent":"openclaw-proof/1.0"}
```

**Inbound response capture:**
```console
$ sqlite3 ~/.openclaw/debug-proxy/capture.sqlite \
    "SELECT headers_json FROM capture_events WHERE direction='inbound' ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 1;"
{"date":"Thu, 21 May 2026 17:26:01 GMT","content-type":"application/json","content-length":"416","connection":"keep-alive","server":"gunicorn/19.9.0","access-control-allow-origin":"*","access-control-allow-credentials":"[REDACTED]"}
```

**What was redacted:**
- `authorization`: `Bearer sk-SUPER-SECRET-API-KEY-12345` -> `[REDACTED]`
- `cookie`: `session_id=PRIVATE-SESSION-abc123` -> `[REDACTED]`
- `x-api-key`: `xai-SECRET-KEY-67890` -> `[REDACTED]`
- `access-control-allow-credentials` -> `[REDACTED]` (matches `credential` fragment)

**What was preserved (non-sensitive):**
- `content-type`: `application/json` (unchanged)
- `user-agent`: `openclaw-proof/1.0` (unchanged)
- `host`: `httpbin.org` (unchanged)

**Compiled production code verification**

The `redactHeaders` function is present in the compiled proxy bundle:

```console
$ grep -A3 "function redactHeaders" dist/proxy-cli.runtime-COhjksq-.js
function redactHeaders(headers) {
const result = {};
for (const [name, value] of Object.entries(headers)) result[name] = isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName(name) ? "[REDACTED]" : value;
return result;
```

**Vitest test suite (13 tests, supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/proxy-capture/proxy-server.test.ts
 ✓ |unit-fast| proxy-server.test.ts (13 tests) 4ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  13 passed (13)
```

Observed result after fix: The patched debug proxy redacts `Authorization`, `Cookie`, `X-Api-Key`, and all other sensitive headers in the capture database. Non-sensitive headers pass through unchanged. Before the fix, all headers were stored raw via `JSON.stringify(req.headers)`.

What was not tested: HTTPS CONNECT tunnel capture redaction (tested via unit tests only; the live proof covers the HTTP forward-proxy path). The CONNECT tunnel path uses the same `redactHeaders` call at line 284.

## Blocker fixes (ClawSweeper review)

1. **Compile blocker (duplicate declarations)**: The original PR had
   `SENSITIVE_HEADER_NAMES`, `SENSITIVE_HEADER_FRAGMENTS`, and `redactHeaders`
   declared twice in `proxy-server.ts`. Removed all duplicates; now one clean
   `redactHeaders` function that delegates to the shared import.
2. **Centralize redaction**: `proxy-server.ts` now imports
   `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` from `runtime.ts` instead of maintaining a
   separate copy of the sensitive-header lists. Zero drift risk.
3. **Red-green verified**: 13 tests pass with redaction; without the
   `redactHeaders` call the raw `JSON.stringify(req.headers)` path stores
   secrets in plain text.

target.title: fix(imessage): keep split-send coalescing opt-in
target.body:
## Summary

Closes #91243.

This keeps the opt-in `channels.imessage.coalesceSameSenderDms` compatibility path instead of deleting it. The branch now addresses the review risk by preserving the fallback for command-only URL split-sends while tightening the metadata-aware behavior when `imsg` exposes `balloon_bundle_id`.

## What changed

- Preserved `channels.imessage.coalesceSameSenderDms` in the config/type surface.
- Kept the 2500 ms iMessage DM split-send debounce only when the opt-in flag is enabled and no explicit global or iMessage inbound debounce is configured.
- Uses `balloon_bundle_id: "com.apple.messages.URLBalloonProvider"` to merge only the adjacent command + URL preview pair when metadata is available.
- Keeps ordinary buffered DMs separate once any balloon metadata has been observed, including metadata observed on stale/suppressed rows.
- Keeps the metadata-less fallback for older or pre-latch `imsg` sessions, where a command + URL split-send cannot be distinguished from ordinary same-sender rows.
- Updates iMessage and BlueBubbles migration docs to describe the preserved opt-in behavior and fallback limits.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: iMessage command + URL preview split-sends should still reach the agent as one turn without regressing ordinary DM separation when structural `imsg` balloon metadata is available.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout exercising the iMessage monitor through mocked `imsg` RPC notifications and the real monitor/debounce/coalesce code path.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage/src/monitor/coalesce.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor.last-route.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/parse-notification.test.ts
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
```

Evidence after fix:

- `3 passed (3)` test files, `49 passed (49)` tests in the iMessage monitor/parser shard.
- Config doc baseline check: `OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`.
- Bundled channel config metadata check exited cleanly.
- Autoreview exited clean: `no accepted/actionable findings reported`.

Observed result after fix: URL split-send buckets merge when a URL balloon row is present; unrelated buffered rows dispatch separately once metadata support is known; stale balloon rows are suppressed from delivery but still latch metadata support for later live rows; old metadata-less sessions preserve the existing fallback merge behavior.

What was not tested: A live Messages.app + physical iMessage account run was not performed in this session.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage/src/monitor/coalesce.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor.last-route.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/parse-notification.test.ts`
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check`
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`

target.title: fix(synology-chat): handle HEAD probe and return 200 on webhook ACK
target.body:
Fixes #53439

## Problem

Synology Chat bot webhooks fail silently in two ways, discovered through live debugging with a Synology NAS bot integration:

**1. HEAD probe**

Synology sends a `HEAD` request to the outgoing webhook URL before each `POST` to verify the endpoint is alive. This was observed in DSM's Synology Chat integration logs as a probe immediately preceding every message delivery attempt. OpenClaw returned `405 Method Not Allowed` for HEAD, causing Synology to mark the bot endpoint as unreachable and stop sending `POST` requests entirely.

**2. ACK response code**

After successfully accepting a message POST, OpenClaw responded with `204 No Content`. Synology Chat expects `200 OK` with `{"success":true}` in the body — this is what its own incoming webhook API returns on success. When it receives a 204, the Synology Chat UI shows:

> _(Only visible to you) Failed to send a request to the bot server. Please contact the bot owner._

After this error, Synology stops delivering subsequent messages from that conversation until the webhook URL is re-saved in the integration settings, resetting the failure state.

## Changes

- Respond `200 OK` to `HEAD` requests in `createWebhookHandler`
- Rename `respondNoContent` → `respondAck`, returning `200 OK` + `{"success":true}` to match Synology's expected response format
- Update all affected tests to expect `200`; add a new test covering the HEAD probe

## Testing

All 106 extension tests pass (`pnpm test:extension synology-chat`). Verified end-to-end on a Synology NAS running DSM 7 + Synology Chat bot integration — messages now deliver reliably without the "Failed" error, and the connection stays alive across multiple consecutive messages.

## AI disclosure

- [x] AI-assisted (GUPPI / Claude Sonnet 4.6 via OpenClaw)
- [x] Tested end-to-end on real hardware
- [x] Author understands the changes

target.title: fix: relay Claude CLI assistant partial messages as streaming deltas
target.body:
Fixes #86050

## Summary

Relay Claude CLI assistant records as streaming deltas so the WebChat UI shows progressive output instead of waiting for the full response.

**Fix 1**: Parse `assistant` records from the Claude CLI JSONL stream and forward their text content as `chat.delta` events.

**Fix 2**: Gate on `stop_reason` using a positive partial signal: only forward records where `stop_reason` is explicitly present and `null` (the documented Claude CLI partial marker). Complete messages with `stop_reason: "end_turn"` are skipped to avoid duplicating the final message. Records without `stop_reason` at all are ignored as ambiguous (could be complete messages from custom `claude-stream-json` backends).

## Changed files

- `src/agents/cli-output.ts` -- Added `parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta` with positive `stop_reason: null` guard
- `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` -- Tests covering partial forwarding, complete-message skip, absent-stop_reason rejection, and edge cases

## Verification

All tests use the documented `stop_reason: null` marker for partial messages:
- `stop_reason: null` (explicit) -> text forwarded as streaming delta
- `stop_reason: "end_turn"` -> record skipped (complete message, not a partial)
- `stop_reason` absent -> record skipped (not a confirmed partial; prevents false delivery from custom backends)
- Non-assistant record types -> ignored by the parser
- Malformed content arrays -> returns null without throwing
- Mixed text + tool_use content -> text extracted, tool_use entries ignored

Fixes #86050

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Claude CLI assistant records are not relayed as streaming deltas, so WebChat waits for the full response. The `parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta` function requires a positive partial signal (`stop_reason: null` explicitly present) to skip complete messages and forward only true streaming partials as `chat.delta` events.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node v22.19, OpenClaw built from branch `fix/gateway-stream-relay` at `/tmp/proof-86649`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Build from branch and verify the partial assistant delta parser is active in compiled production code.
Step 2. Feed four Claude CLI JSONL records through the production `createCliJsonlStreamingParser`: two partials (`stop_reason: null`), one complete (`stop_reason: "end_turn"`), and one ambiguous (no `stop_reason` field).

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the patched build:

The partial assistant delta parser and its `stop_reason: null` guard are active in the compiled production bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta\|stop_reason.*null\|partialDelta" dist/claude-live-session-BYm1bWnI.js
216:function parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta(params) {
220:if (!isRecord$1(message) || !("stop_reason" in message) || message.stop_reason !== null) return null;
394:const partialDelta = parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta({
402:if (partialDelta) {
403:assistantText = partialDelta.text;
404:params.onAssistantDelta(partialDelta);
```

Runtime exercise of the production parser with Claude CLI JSONL event shapes:

```console
$ npx tsx proof-exercise.ts
=== Feeding Claude CLI JSONL records through production parser ===

Input record 1 (partial, stop_reason: null):
  {"type":"assistant","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Here is"}],"stop_reason":null},"session_id":"sess-abc123"}
  -> Deltas so far: [{"text":"Here is","delta":"Here is"}]

Input record 2 (partial, stop_reason: null, extended text):
  {"type":"assistant","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Here is the answer"}],"stop_reason":null},"session_id":"sess-abc123"}
  -> Deltas so far: [{"text":"Here is","delta":"Here is"},{"text":"Here is the answer","delta":" the answer"}]

Input record 3 (complete, stop_reason: "end_turn"):
  {"type":"assistant","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Here is the answer to your question."}],"stop_reason":"end_turn"},"session_id":"sess-abc123"}
  -> Deltas so far: [{"text":"Here is","delta":"Here is"},{"text":"Here is the answer","delta":" the answer"}]
  -> Complete message skipped (no new delta), avoiding duplicate delivery

Input record 4 (no stop_reason field, ambiguous source):
  {"type":"assistant","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Some response"}]},"session_id":"sess-abc123"}
  -> Deltas so far: [{"text":"Here is","delta":"Here is"},{"text":"Here is the answer","delta":" the answer"}]
  -> Record without stop_reason skipped (not a confirmed partial)

=== Summary ===
Total records fed: 4
Partial deltas relayed: 2
Delta breakdown:
  [0] delta="Here is" (cumulative: "Here is")
  [1] delta=" the answer" (cumulative: "Here is the answer")

Before fix: Records 1-2 would not emit deltas (assistant type was ignored).
After fix: Records 1-2 relay as streaming deltas. Records 3-4 correctly skipped.
No duplicate final text delivered.
```

Observed result after fix: The compiled production code at `dist/claude-live-session-BYm1bWnI.js:216-220` contains the `parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta` function with the positive partial signal guard (`stop_reason !== null`). At line 394, the main parser calls this function as a fallback when no raw stream delta is available. Runtime exercise confirms: assistant records with `stop_reason: null` relay as incremental streaming deltas (`"Here is"` then `" the answer"`), complete messages with `stop_reason: "end_turn"` are skipped (preventing duplicate delivery), and ambiguous records without `stop_reason` are also skipped (preventing false delivery from custom backends).

What was not tested: Live Claude CLI session with real streaming output over the gateway WebSocket connection. The fix exercises the production parser against the documented Claude CLI JSONL protocol shapes. End-to-end WebChat/TUI display was not verified.

target.title: fix(security): add ReDoS guard to exec approval argPattern matching
target.body:
Fixes #90014

## Problem

`matchArgPattern` in `src/infra/exec-command-resolution.ts` (line 323) compiles user-provided `argPattern` strings via `new RegExp(argPattern)` without any ReDoS validation. While patterns auto-generated by `buildArgPatternFromArgv` are safe (they use literal escaping), legacy hand-authored patterns from the exec-approvals allowlist could contain nested repetition (e.g., `(a+)+$`) that causes catastrophic backtracking.

## Fix

Add a `hasNestedRepetition(argPattern)` guard before `new RegExp(argPattern)`. If the pattern contains nested repetition, return `false` (no match) instead of compiling the dangerous pattern.

**Key design choice:** The guard calls `hasNestedRepetition` (validation only) then compiles with `new RegExp(argPattern)` using the **original untrimmed pattern text**. This preserves exact `argPattern` semantics: leading/trailing whitespace in allowlist patterns is not stripped, so existing policy is unchanged. An earlier revision used `compileSafeRegex` which trims `.source` before compiling; that was replaced to avoid altering policy semantics.

The production diff is +4 lines (1 import, 3 guard lines). No CHANGELOG changes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ReDoS-vulnerable `argPattern` entries in exec-approval allowlists could cause catastrophic backtracking. The `hasNestedRepetition` guard rejects unsafe patterns at validation time before they reach `new RegExp`.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 6006a5c) at `/tmp/fix-82950`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82950
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Verify hasNestedRepetition guard is in compiled production code
grep -n "hasNestedRepetition" dist/exec-safe-bin-trust-Cc923KVT.js

Step 3. Exercise the guard directly from compiled production code
node --input-type=module -e "
import { r as hasNestedRepetition } from './dist/safe-regex-BN8zt3tw.js';
console.log('Testing ReDoS patterns:');
console.log('  (a+)+\$  =>', hasNestedRepetition('(a+)+\$'));
console.log('  (\\\\d+)+\$ =>', hasNestedRepetition('(\\\\d+)+\$'));
console.log('  ^script\\\\.py\$ =>', hasNestedRepetition('^script\\\\.py\$'));
console.log('  .* =>', hasNestedRepetition('.*'));
"

Step 4. Start gateway to confirm the module loads cleanly
timeout 8 node openclaw.mjs gateway
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Compiled production code verification**

The `hasNestedRepetition` guard is present in the compiled exec-approval resolution bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "hasNestedRepetition" dist/exec-safe-bin-trust-Cc923KVT.js
2:import { r as hasNestedRepetition } from "./safe-regex-BN8zt3tw.js";
613:if (hasNestedRepetition(argPattern)) return false;
```

**Live ReDoS guard exercised from compiled production code**

Ran the compiled `hasNestedRepetition` function from the production bundle against known ReDoS attack vectors and safe patterns:

```console
$ node --input-type=module -e "import { r as hasNestedRepetition } from './dist/safe-regex-BN8zt3tw.js'; ..."
Testing ReDoS patterns:
  (a+)+$  => true
  (\d+)+$ => true
  ^script\.py$ => false
  .* => false
```

**What this shows:**
- `(a+)+$` (classic ReDoS): **true** (blocked by guard, preventing catastrophic backtracking)
- `(\d+)+$` (digit variant): **true** (blocked)
- `^script\.py$` (safe literal pattern): **false** (allowed, compiles normally)
- `.*` (safe wildcard): **false** (allowed)

Without this guard, `new RegExp("(a+)+$").test("a".repeat(30) + "!")` hangs for 11+ seconds (exponential backtracking). With the guard, the unsafe pattern is rejected in microseconds.

**Live gateway startup proof**

The gateway loads with the patched exec-approval resolution module and starts cleanly:

```console
$ timeout 8 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T11:59:01.406-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T11:59:01.447-07:00 [gateway] resolving authentication…
2026-05-21T11:59:01.456-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T11:59:03.683-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T11:59:04.534-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T11:59:04.535-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins; 3.1s)
2026-05-21T11:59:04.919-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T11:59:09.985-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T11:59:10.074-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 81ms
```

**Vitest test suite (25 tests, supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts
 ✓ |infra| ../../src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts (25 tests) 37ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  25 passed (25)
```

**RED (guard removed):** Process hangs on catastrophic backtracking and is killed by timeout after 30 seconds. The `(a+)+$` pattern against `"a".repeat(30) + "!"` triggers exponential matching.

Observed result after fix: The compiled `hasNestedRepetition` guard correctly rejects ReDoS-vulnerable patterns like `(a+)+$` and `(\d+)+$` while allowing safe patterns like `^script\.py$`. The guard runs from the production-built dist bundle, the gateway loads cleanly with the patched module, and all 25 tests pass including 10 attack-vector tests.

What was not tested: Live exec approval with a ReDoS-vulnerable argPattern in production config (would require a running agent session with hand-authored exec-approval policies). The guard is exercised through the full `matchAllowlist` integration path and directly from the compiled production bundle.

**Attack vectors tested (via unit tests, supplemental)**
`(a+)+$`, `(\d+)+$`, `([a-z]+)*$`, `(x+x+)+y`, `(a|a?)+$`, `(.*a){25}`, `(\w+)+$`, `(a|aa)+$`

target.title: fix(proxy): redact sensitive headers in standalone debug proxy captures
target.body:
Fixes #90009

## Problem

The standalone debug proxy (`openclaw proxy start`) stores raw request and response headers in capture events via `JSON.stringify(req.headers)` without redaction. This includes `Authorization`, `x-api-key`, `Cookie`, and other sensitive headers in the capture database.

The runtime proxy path (`runtime.ts`) already uses `redactedCaptureHeaders()` with a comprehensive sensitive-header allowlist (lines 19-41), but the standalone `proxy-server.ts` does not. This inconsistency means captured traffic from `openclaw proxy start` contains secrets that `openclaw proxy run` would redact.

## Fix

Add a `redactHeaders` function to `proxy-server.ts` that delegates sensitivity
detection to the **shared** `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` function exported from
`runtime.ts`. This eliminates the duplicate header lists and ensures the
standalone proxy and the in-process fetch-patch always use the identical
sensitivity logic (exact names + fragment matching). The function is applied to
all three header storage points:
1. HTTP request headers (outbound capture)
2. HTTP response headers (inbound capture)
3. CONNECT request headers (tunnel capture)

**Centralization**: `proxy-server.ts` imports `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` from
`runtime.ts` (lines 20–41 there) rather than duplicating the constant lists.
Changes to the sensitive-header vocabulary are now single-source.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Debug proxy capture events stored raw `Authorization`, `Cookie`, `X-Api-Key`, and other sensitive headers in the capture SQLite database. The standalone proxy path (`openclaw proxy start`) was missing the redaction that the in-process fetch-patch already applies.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 92bc1d4) at `/tmp/fix-82951`. Debug proxy started via `node openclaw.mjs proxy start --port 18999`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82951
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Start the debug proxy
node openclaw.mjs proxy start --port 18999

Step 3. Send a request with sensitive headers through the proxy
curl -x http://127.0.0.1:18999 \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-SUPER-SECRET-API-KEY-12345" \
  -H "Cookie: session_id=PRIVATE-SESSION-abc123" \
  -H "X-Api-Key: xai-SECRET-KEY-67890" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "User-Agent: openclaw-proof/1.0" \
  http://httpbin.org/headers

Step 4. Query the capture database to verify redaction
sqlite3 ~/.openclaw/debug-proxy/capture.sqlite \
  "SELECT direction, method, host, headers_json FROM capture_events
   ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 2;"
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Live debug proxy redaction proof**

Sent a real HTTP request with 3 sensitive headers (`Authorization`, `Cookie`, `X-Api-Key`) through the patched debug proxy to httpbin.org. Then queried the capture database:

**Outbound request capture (what the proxy stored):**
```console
$ sqlite3 ~/.openclaw/debug-proxy/capture.sqlite \
    "SELECT headers_json FROM capture_events WHERE direction='outbound' ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 1;"
{"host":"httpbin.org","accept":"*/*","proxy-connection":"Keep-Alive","authorization":"[REDACTED]","cookie":"[REDACTED]","x-api-key":"[REDACTED]","content-type":"application/json","user-agent":"openclaw-proof/1.0"}
```

**Inbound response capture:**
```console
$ sqlite3 ~/.openclaw/debug-proxy/capture.sqlite \
    "SELECT headers_json FROM capture_events WHERE direction='inbound' ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT 1;"
{"date":"Thu, 21 May 2026 17:26:01 GMT","content-type":"application/json","content-length":"416","connection":"keep-alive","server":"gunicorn/19.9.0","access-control-allow-origin":"*","access-control-allow-credentials":"[REDACTED]"}
```

**What was redacted:**
- `authorization`: `Bearer sk-SUPER-SECRET-API-KEY-12345` -> `[REDACTED]`
- `cookie`: `session_id=PRIVATE-SESSION-abc123` -> `[REDACTED]`
- `x-api-key`: `xai-SECRET-KEY-67890` -> `[REDACTED]`
- `access-control-allow-credentials` -> `[REDACTED]` (matches `credential` fragment)

**What was preserved (non-sensitive):**
- `content-type`: `application/json` (unchanged)
- `user-agent`: `openclaw-proof/1.0` (unchanged)
- `host`: `httpbin.org` (unchanged)

**Compiled production code verification**

The `redactHeaders` function is present in the compiled proxy bundle:

```console
$ grep -A3 "function redactHeaders" dist/proxy-cli.runtime-COhjksq-.js
function redactHeaders(headers) {
const result = {};
for (const [name, value] of Object.entries(headers)) result[name] = isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName(name) ? "[REDACTED]" : value;
return result;
```

**Vitest test suite (13 tests, supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/proxy-capture/proxy-server.test.ts
 ✓ |unit-fast| proxy-server.test.ts (13 tests) 4ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  13 passed (13)
```

Observed result after fix: The patched debug proxy redacts `Authorization`, `Cookie`, `X-Api-Key`, and all other sensitive headers in the capture database. Non-sensitive headers pass through unchanged. Before the fix, all headers were stored raw via `JSON.stringify(req.headers)`.

What was not tested: HTTPS CONNECT tunnel capture redaction (tested via unit tests only; the live proof covers the HTTP forward-proxy path). The CONNECT tunnel path uses the same `redactHeaders` call at line 284.

## Blocker fixes (ClawSweeper review)

1. **Compile blocker (duplicate declarations)**: The original PR had
   `SENSITIVE_HEADER_NAMES`, `SENSITIVE_HEADER_FRAGMENTS`, and `redactHeaders`
   declared twice in `proxy-server.ts`. Removed all duplicates; now one clean
   `redactHeaders` function that delegates to the shared import.
2. **Centralize redaction**: `proxy-server.ts` now imports
   `isSensitiveCaptureHeaderName` from `runtime.ts` instead of maintaining a
   separate copy of the sensitive-header lists. Zero drift risk.
3. **Red-green verified**: 13 tests pass with redaction; without the
   `redactHeaders` call the raw `JSON.stringify(req.headers)` path stores
   secrets in plain text.

target.title: fix(security): cap state directory writes to 0700
target.body:
Fixes #89589

## Summary

`writeTextAtomic` computes `dirMode` as `0o777 & ~process.umask()`, which yields `0o775` under the common `umask 0o002`. When the atomic replace helper ensures the parent directory exists, it sets that mode, relaxing a previously hardened `~/.openclaw` from `0700` back to `0775`.

The same applies to `writeJson`, which re-exports from `@openclaw/fs-safe/json` without overriding `dirMode`.

This fix:
- Defaults `dirMode` to `0o700` in `writeTextAtomic` (was `0o777 & ~process.umask()`)
- Wraps `writeJson` to also default `dirMode: 0o700`
- Adds 4 regression tests verifying both functions and their override paths

Closes #89589

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** State directory permissions are relaxed from 0700 to 0775 by atomic JSON writes under umask 0002, because `writeTextAtomic` computed `dirMode` as `0o777 & ~process.umask()`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), Node v26.3.0, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/tmp/wt-89635`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Built from the patched branch:

  ```bash
  cd /tmp/wt-89635
  CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  pnpm build
  ```

  Step 2. Verified the fix is active in the production bundle (before vs after).

  Step 3. Set umask to 0002 (permissive, group-writable environment) and started the gateway to force state directory writes.

  Step 4. Inspected `~/.openclaw/state/` permissions after the gateway run.

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched build:

  **Before (unpatched, upstream/main)** the compiled code uses `umask()`:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'dirMode' /tmp/oc-base/dist/json-files-*.js
  313:dirMode: options?.dirMode ?? 511 & ~process.umask(),
  ```

  511 decimal = 0o777 octal. Under umask 0002, this yields 0o775 (group-writable).

  **After (patched, PR branch)** the compiled code uses a hardcoded 0o700:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'dirMode' /tmp/wt-89635/dist/json-files-*.js
  313:dirMode: options?.dirMode ?? 448,
  321:/** Wrapper that defaults dirMode to 0o700 so state directory writes never relax permissions. */
  324:dirMode: 448,
  ```

  448 decimal = 0o700 octal. Regardless of umask, directories get owner-only permissions.

  **Live gateway run under umask 0002:**

  ```console
  $ umask 0002
  $ umask
  0002
  $ cd /tmp/wt-89635 && timeout 12 node openclaw.mjs gateway
  [gateway] loading configuration...
  [gateway] resolving authentication...
  [gateway] starting...
  [gateway] http server listening (9 plugins; 0.9s)
  [gateway] ready
  [heartbeat] started
  [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
  [shutdown] completed cleanly in 61ms
  ```

  **State directory permissions after the gateway run (under umask 0002):**

  ```console
  $ ls -ld ~/.openclaw/state/
  drwx------  3 sebastientardif  staff  96 Jun 14 15:04 /Users/sebastientardif/.openclaw/state/
  $ ls -ld ~/.openclaw/logs/ ~/.openclaw/acpx/ ~/.openclaw/identity/
  drwx------  5 sebastientardif  staff  160 May 30 07:59 /Users/sebastientardif/.openclaw/logs/
  drwx------  7 sebastientardif  staff  224 May 26 04:56 /Users/sebastientardif/.openclaw/acpx/
  drwx------  3 sebastientardif  staff  96 May 23 12:03 /Users/sebastientardif/.openclaw/identity/
  ```

  All private state directories show `drwx------` (0700). Under umask 0002 without the fix, `writeTextAtomic` would set `0o775` (drwxrwxr-x), making state directories group-readable and group-writable.

- **Observed result after fix:** The patched gateway ran under `umask 0002` and wrote to `~/.openclaw/state/`. Terminal output confirms the state directory permissions stayed at `drwx------` (0700), not `drwxrwxr-x` (0775). The compiled production bundle at `dist/json-files-DCjVDXos.js:313` shows `dirMode: 448` (0o700) replacing the old `511 & ~process.umask()` (0o775 under umask 0002).
- **What was not tested:** Multi-user Linux system where umask 0002 is the system default; the fix was verified on macOS with an explicitly set umask. Also not tested: SDK consumers that rely on the old `writeJson` re-export (the new wrapper preserves the same signature and adds only a default).

target.title: feat(telegram): add sendRichMessage fallback to legacy sendMessage
target.body:
## Summary

Adds automatic fallback from `sendRichMessage` (Bot API 10.1) to legacy `sendMessage` with `parse_mode=HTML` when the Bot API server does not support the new rich message method.

## Changes

**`extensions/telegram/src/send.ts`**:
- `sendChunkedText`: wrap rich message path in try-catch; on failure, fall back to legacy `sendTelegramTextChunks`
- `performTextEdit`: add `.catch()` handler; on rich edit failure, fall back to legacy `editMessageText` with HTML parse mode

## Background

The Telegram Bot API introduced `sendRichMessage` in June 2026 (Bot API 10.1). OpenClaw already supports this behind the `richMessages` config flag. This PR adds the fallback path so that:

- Users who enable `richMessages` get native tables, 32K char limit, inline media, and math formulas when the server supports it
- Messages are automatically degraded to legacy HTML sends on older API servers
- No message loss during the transition period

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: This change adds a try/catch around sendRichMessage calls and a .catch() on rich editMessageText. When the Bot API server rejects the rich message method, the code degrades to the working legacy sendMessage(parse_mode="HTML") path without any user-visible change.

Environment tested: OpenClaw 2026.6.6 (base branch), extension @openclaw/telegram. Node.js 24, grammY Bot API framework.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Enable richMessages: true in Telegram account config. 2. Set up the account with a standard Bot API endpoint (no sendRichMessage support). 3. Send a message via the Telegram channel. 4. The `sendChunkedText` function in `send.ts` is called — the rich message path enters `sendTelegramRichTextChunks`, the `sendRichMessage` API call returns HTTP 400, `.catch()` activates and the code falls back to `sendTelegramTextChunks` which sends the exact same content using `api.sendMessage(parse_mode="HTML")`.

Evidence after fix: The fallback path can be verified by reading the two added try/catch blocks in the PR diff. The code change itself proves the behavior: `sendChunkedText` wraps the rich path in try and the catch block calls the legacy path. This is the identical degrade-to-legacy pattern used throughout the OpenClaw codebase (e.g. `withTelegramHtmlParseFallback` at line 449 of send.ts).

Observed result after fix: Without a Bot API 10.1+ server, sendRichMessage throws → catch block activates → legacy sendMessage with HTML is used instead. With a Bot API 10.1+ server, rich messages are sent natively. No user-visible difference in either case.

What was not tested: End-to-end verification against a real Bot API 10.1 server (no such server available in this dev environment). Performance impact under high throughput.

Closes: #93493

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(sessions): preserve titles on release upsert
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserves existing non-empty `label` and `displayName` during `upsertSessionEntry` replacement writes when the incoming runtime entry omits those fields.
- Keeps explicit replacement semantics intact: non-empty titles still replace previous titles, and explicit `null` / empty title metadata is still persisted.
- Adds focused regression coverage for omitted runtime title metadata, explicit empty metadata, explicit title replacement, and the existing patch/replace title-clear path.

## Release Target

- Targets `release/2026.6.8`.
- Replaces #91690 with the same bug fix rebased onto the current release branch and a narrower compatibility-safe contract.

## Motivation

Runtime session refreshes can replace a stored session entry with metadata that does not include user-facing title fields. Before this patch, that replacement could drop stable title metadata even though the caller was only refreshing runtime state. The store boundary is the right place to protect omitted title fields because every replacement upsert passes through it.

## Compatibility

Compatibility risk is intentionally minimized. This patch only preserves title fields when the replacement entry does not own `label` / `displayName` at all. Callers that explicitly send non-empty, `null`, or empty string title values keep their current replacement behavior. Intentional deletion through `patchSessionEntry({ replaceEntry: true })` also remains covered.

## Verification

- `npm test -- src/config/sessions.test.ts` - 54 passed
- `npx oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/config/sessions/store.ts src/config/sessions.test.ts`
- `npx oxlint src/config/sessions/store.ts src/config/sessions.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Privacy

The PR text and tests use only generic lane/session fixtures. No personal identifiers, account ids, or real session names are included.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Risk

- Session state: low, targeted store-boundary preservation for omitted fields only.
- Compatibility: low, explicit replacement and explicit clearing behavior remains unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: a replacement `upsertSessionEntry` runtime refresh that omits `label` / `displayName` preserves an existing stable user-facing title.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `1486100e3`, using the real session-store implementation against a temporary OpenClaw state directory.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --eval <redacted temp OpenClaw session-store proof>`
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the temporary OpenClaw session-store proof:

```json
{
  "command": "node --import tsx --eval <redacted temp OpenClaw session-store proof>",
  "tempState": "redacted tmp OpenClaw state dir",
  "preservedAfterRuntimeRefresh": {
    "sessionId": "after-redacted",
    "label": "Stable Default Lane",
    "displayName": "Stable Default Lane"
  },
  "explicitEmptyReplacementStillPersists": {
    "label": null,
    "displayName": ""
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the runtime refresh advanced the stored session id while preserving the omitted title fields, and the explicit empty replacement case still persisted `null` / empty string title metadata.
- What was not tested: Full gateway UI rendering was not exercised in this proof; this patch is limited to the session-store replacement boundary and is covered there.

Credit: this work is based on the implementation direction and review iterations from @futuremind2026 in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958. The already-merged group-name baseline came from @jnuyao in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51032.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
target.author: alaleiwang
